      β-carotene
        Carotenoderma (palms/soles yellow, sclerae white)
        Smokers: ↑lung cancer (CARET/ATBC)

9. 9. -Hour Recall Prompts

  1. Draw visual cycle: photon → 11-cis-retinal → all-trans → hyperpolarisation → regeneration in RPE
  2. List WHO ocular classification X1A through XS
  3. WHO prophylaxis doses by age; treatment protocol (3 doses)
  4. Retinoic acid mechanism: RAR/RXR → HOX genes → epithelial differentiation
  5. Hypervitaminosis A: acute vs chronic features; teratogenicity window
  6. β-carotene: carotenoderma vs jaundice; CARET/ATBC smoker warning
  7. RAE conversions: 1 µg retinol = 1 RAE = 12 µg β-carotene = 24 µg other
  8. Isotretinoin: Category X, iPledge, side effects (cheilitis, ↑TG, ↑LFT, mood)

10. 10. -Day / 15-Day / 30-Day Revision Tracker

DayDateRecall Quality (1-5)Time SpentNotes
1
7
15
30

11. 11. Must Know / Should Know / Nice to Know

PriorityContent
Must Know 🔴Visual cycle (rhodopsin), WHO ocular classification X1A–X3B, night blindness → keratomalacia progression, WHO prophylaxis/treatment doses, teratogenicity (isotretinoin Cat X), chronic toxicity triad (hepatomegaly, hyperostosis, PIVS), RAE conversion
Should Know 🟡BCO1 polymorphism, measles mortality reduction, post-partum supplementation, carotenoderma vs jaundice, CARET/ATBC trial implications, RBP transport
Nice to Know 🟢Isotretinoin iPledge details, corneal transplant for XS, RPE65 mutation (Leber congenital amaurosis), retinoic acid syndrome (APL treatment)

12. 12. My Weak Points

  • [ ] Exact molecular steps of visual transduction (transducin, PDE, cGMP)
  • [ ] RBP-transthyretin stoichiometry and STRA6 receptor details
  • [ ] iPledge programme specific requirements

13. 13. Self-Test Scorecard

DomainScore /10Target /10
Understanding8+
Recall8+
MCQ Performance8+
SBA Performance8+
Viva Confidence8+
TOTAL40+/50

14. 14. Exam Answer Modes

1. Long Answer / Essay (20 min)

Topic: "Vitamin A: metabolism, deficiency manifestations, and management"

  • Forms: retinol, retinal, retinoic acid, β-carotene; absorption, transport (RBP), storage
  • Visual cycle: 11-cis-retinal + opsin → rhodopsin; light → all-trans → hyperpolarisation; regeneration
  • Epithelial differentiation: retinoic acid → RAR/RXR → HOX genes
  • Deficiency: WHO classification (X1A night blindness → X3B keratomalacia → XS blindness); systemic (phrynoderma, growth, immunity)
  • WHO prevention: 100k IU (6–12m), 200k IU (12–59m) q4–6m; treatment day 1, 2, 14
  • Toxicity: acute (nausea, bulging fontanelle), chronic (hepatomegaly, hyperostosis, PIVS), teratogenicity (isotretinoin Cat X, HOX genes, weeks 3–8)
  • β-carotene: carotenoderma (harmless), smokers ↑lung cancer

2. Short Note (7 min)

Topic: "Xerophthalmia classification and management"

  • X1A: Night blindness (impaired dark adaptation)
  • X1B: Bitot's spots (foamy temporal conjunctiva) + conjunctival xerosis
  • X2: Corneal xerosis (dull, hazy cornea)
  • X3A: Keratomalacia <1/3 cornea; X3B: >1/3 (perforation risk)
  • XS: Corneal scar (blindness)
  • Treatment: 200k IU (100k if 6–12m) Day 1, Day 2, Day 14; topical antibiotics, eye padding for keratomalacia

3. Viva Answer (3 min)

Q: "A pregnant woman at 10 weeks takes isotretinoin for acne. Risk?"

"A: Category X teratogen. Retinoic acid → HOX gene dysregulation → neural crest defects (craniofacial, cardiac, CNS, thymic). Critical window: weeks 3–8. Absolute contraindication; must stop 1 month before conception; iPledge programme mandates 2 negative pregnancy tests + 2 contraception methods."

4. Ward Case Discussion (5 min)

Case: 2y child, measles, night blindness, Bitot's spots, weight 9 kg.

"Immediate: Vitamin A 200,000 IU oral Day 1, Day 2, Day 14 (WHO). Treat measles complications (pneumonia, diarrhoea, otitis). Nutritional rehabilitation. Counsel mother: liver, eggs, orange vegetables. Follow-up ophthalmology for corneal status."

5. Last-Night-Before-Exam Sheet (1 min)

  • Forms: Retinol (transport), Retinal (vision), Retinoic acid (genes), β-carotene (provitamin)
  • Visual cycle: 11-cis + opsin → rhodopsin → photon → all-trans → hyperpolarisation
  • WHO X-class: X1A night blind → X1B Bitot's/xerosis → X2 corneal xerosis → X3A/B keratomalacia → XS scar/blindness
  • Prophylaxis: 100k IU (6–12m), 200k IU (12–59m) q4–6m
  • Treatment: 200k IU Day 1, Day 2, Day 14
  • Toxicity: Acute (nausea, bulging fontanelle); Chronic (hepatomegaly, hyperostosis, PIVS); Teratogenic (isotretinoin Cat X)
  • β-carotene: Carotenoderma (palms yellow, sclerae white); smokers ↑lung cancer
  • RAE: 1 µg retinol = 1 RAE = 12 µg β-carotene = 24 µg other

15. 15. MCQs (10)

  1. Visual chromophore in rhodopsin is:

A. Retinol

B. 11-cis-retinal

C. Retinoic acid

D. β-carotene

  1. WHO classification for night blindness is:

A. X1B

B. X1A

C. X2

D. X3A

  1. Bitot's spots are:

A. Corneal ulcers

B. Foamy conjunctival deposits (temporal)

C. Retinal haemorrhages

D. Lens opacities

  1. Vitamin A treatment dose for xerophthalmia in 2-year-old (WHO):

A. 50,000 IU day 1 only

B. 100,000 IU day 1, 2, 14

C. 200,000 IU day 1, 2, 14

D. 200,000 IU single dose

  1. Teratogenicity of isotretinoin is due to:

A. ↑Retinol storage

B. HOX gene dysregulation

C. Rhodopsin mutation

D. ↑ICP

  1. Critical gestational window for retinoid teratogenicity:

A. Weeks 1–2

B. Weeks 3–8

C. Weeks 12–16

D. Weeks 20–24

  1. Chronic hypervitaminosis A features INCLUDE:

A. Night blindness

B. Hepatomegaly, hyperostosis, PIVS

C. Keratomalacia

D. Scurvy

  1. β-carotene excess causes:

A. Jaundice (sclerae yellow)

B. Carotenoderma (palms/soles yellow, sclerae white)

C. Keratomalacia

D. Hepatomegaly

  1. RAE conversion: 1 µg β-carotene = ? µg RAE

A. 1/24

B. 1/12

C. 1/6

D. 1/2

16. 16. SBA Questions (5)

  1. A 28-year-old woman on isotretinoin for severe acne discovers she is 6 weeks pregnant. Most appropriate action?

A. Reduce isotretinoin dose

B. Stop isotretinoin immediately; refer for fetal anomaly scan; counsel teratogenic risk (Category X)

C. Switch to topical tretinoin

D. Continue isotretinoin with high-dose folic acid

E. Delay pregnancy test until 12 weeks

  1. An 18-month-old child presents with inability to see in dim light and foamy triangular deposits on temporal conjunctivae. Weight 8 kg. Next step?

A. Refer for corneal transplant

B. Vitamin A 200,000 IU oral Day 1, Day 2, Day 14

C. Topical vitamin A eye drops only

D. High-dose β-carotene supplementation

E. Retinol 100,000 IU IM single dose

  1. A 45-year-old man taking vitamin A supplement 50,000 IU daily for 2 years presents with bone pain, dry skin, and headache. Fundoscopy shows papilloedema. Likely diagnosis?

A. Acute hypervitaminosis A

B. Chronic hypervitaminosis A with pseudotumor cerebri

C. Vitamin A deficiency

D. β-carotene toxicity

E. Multiple myeloma

  1. A pregnant woman at 12 weeks gestation asks about vitamin A supplementation for night blindness. Safe upper limit?

A. 200,000 IU single dose

B. 100,000 IU daily

C. 10,000 IU/day (or 25,000 IU/week) preformed vitamin A

D. 50,000 IU weekly

E. β-carotene 20 mg/day preferred

  1. A 6-month-old infant with cystic fibrosis has steatorrhea and poor weight gain. Which fat-soluble vitamin requires water-miscible preparation?

A. Vitamin D only

B. Vitamin K only

C. Vitamin A (and D, E, K all require water-miscible forms)

D. Vitamin E only

E. None (pancreatic enzymes sufficient)

17. 17. Flashcards

  • Q: Visual chromophore

A: 11-cis-retinal (bound to opsin = rhodopsin)

  • Q: Night blindness WHO code

A: X1A

  • Q: Bitot's spots location/appearance

A: Temporal bulbar conjunctiva, foamy/triangular

  • Q: Keratomalacia X3A vs X3B

A: X3A <1/3 cornea; X3B >1/3 (perforation risk)

  • Q: WHO treatment protocol (child >12m)

A: 200,000 IU Day 1, Day 2, Day 14

  • Q: Retinoic acid mechanism

A: RAR/RXR nuclear receptor → HOX gene transcription → epithelial differentiation

  • Q: Teratogenicity window

A: Weeks 3–8 gestation (neural crest)

  • Q: Isotretinoin Category

A: X (absolute contraindication in pregnancy); iPledge programme

  • Q: Acute toxicity features

A: Nausea, vomiting, headache, bulging fontanelle (infants)

  • Q: Chronic toxicity triad

A: Hepatomegaly, hyperostosis, PIVS (pseudotumor cerebri)

  • Q: Carotenoderma vs jaundice

A: Carotenoderma: palms/soles yellow, sclerae WHITE; Jaundice: sclerae yellow

  • Q: β-carotene smokers

A: CARET/ATBC: ↑lung cancer mortality; avoid high-dose β-carotene in smokers

  • Q: RAE conversions

A: 1 µg retinol = 1 RAE = 12 µg β-carotene = 24 µg α-carotene/β-cryptoxanthin

  • Q: Prophylaxis dose (6–12m / 12–59m)

A: 100k IU / 200k IU q4–6m

  • Q: Postpartum dose

A: 200k IU single dose within 8 weeks

18. 18. Answer Key with Explanations

1. MCQs

  1. B — 11-cis-retinal is the chromophore; retinol is transport/storage; retinoic acid is nuclear signalling; β-carotene is provitamin.
  2. B — X1A = night blindness; X1B = Bitot's/conjunctival xerosis; X2 = corneal xerosis; X3 = keratomalacia.
  3. B — Bitot's spots: foamy, triangular, temporal conjunctival deposits of keratinised epithelium.
  4. C — WHO: 200k IU day 1, 2, 14 for children >12m; 100k IU for 6–12m.
  5. B — Retinoic acid → RAR/RXR → HOX gene transcription → craniofacial/cardiac/CNS defects.
  6. B — Weeks 3–8 = organogenesis, neural crest migration.
  7. B — Chronic: hepatomegaly, hyperostosis (long bones), PIVS (↑ICP, papilloedema), alopecia, dry skin.
  8. B — Carotenoderma: yellow palms/soles, sclerae white; harmless, reversible.
  9. A — 1 µg β-carotene = 1/12 µg RAE (since 12 µg β-carotene = 1 µg RAE).

2. SBAs

  1. B — Category X, absolute contraindication; stop immediately; fetal anomaly scan; counsel risk.
  2. B — X1A (night blindness) + X1B (Bitot's) = xerophthalmia; WHO treatment: 200k IU day 1, 2, 14.
  3. B — Chronic toxicity: bone pain (hyperostosis), headache/papilloedema (PIVS), dry skin; 50k IU/day ×2y = chronic.
  4. C — Pregnancy: max 10k IU/day preformed vit A; high-dose teratogenic; β-carotene preferred source.
  5. C — CF: fat malabsorption → all fat-soluble vitamins (A, D, E, K) deficient; need water-miscible preparations.

19. 19. Summary

Vitamin A is a Must Know 🔴 topic for FCPS/MRCP.

Key takeaway: Vitamin A (retinol/retinal/retinoic acid/β-carotene) essential for vision (rhodopsin cycle), epithelial integrity (retinoic acid → RAR/RXR → HOX), immunity. Deficiency = xerophthalmia (X1A night blindness → X3B keratomalacia → XS blindness) + phrynoderma + growth failure + ↑infection mortality. WHO: prophylaxis 100k/200k IU q4–6m; treatment 200k day 1,2,14. Toxicity: chronic (hepatomegaly, hyperostosis, PIVS), teratogenic (isotretinoin Cat X, HOX genes, weeks 3–8). β-carotene: carotenoderma only; smokers ↑lung cancer.

Exam focus: Visual cycle, WHO classification/treatment, teratogenicity window, toxicity triad, RAE conversions.

Clinical relevance: Measles mortality reduction; VAD prevention in preschool children; isotretinoin counselling; CF fat-soluble vitamin supplementation.

Template version: 1.0 | Davidson 24e Ch 22 aligned | FCPS/MRCP oriented

topics.title: Vitamin D Metabolism Deficiency Osteomalacia
topics.html:
Related: [[Nutritional Factors in Disease MOC]], [[Davidson Chapter 22 - Nutritional Factors in Disease Hierarchy]], [[../00_Index/Medicine MOC|Medicine MOC]]

[!important]
Vitamin D (cholecalciferol D3/ergocalciferol D2) → liver 25-hydroxylase → 25(OH)D (storage) → kidney 1α-hydroxylase → 1,25(OH)2D (active hormone); deficiency = rickets (children) / osteomalacia (adults); optimal 25(OH)D >30 ng/mL.

1. 1. Learning Objectives

  • [ ] Describe vitamin D synthesis (UVB → 7-dehydrocholesterol → pre-D3 → D3) and dietary sources (D2 fungi, D3 animal)
  • [ ] Explain two-step hydroxylation: liver CYP2R1 (25-hydroxylase) → 25(OH)D; kidney CYP27B1 (1α-hydroxylase) → 1,25(OH)2D
  • [ ] List regulators of 1α-hydroxylase: ↑PTH, ↓Ca, ↓PO4, ↑FGF23? (complex), growth factors; inhibitors: ↑Ca, ↑PO4, ↑FGF23, ↓PTH
  • [ ] Define vitamin D status: deficiency <12 ng/mL, insufficiency 12–20, sufficient 20–30, optimal >30 ng/mL (Endocrine Society)
  • [ ] Identify rickets signs: craniotabes, rachitic rosary, Harrison's groove, bowed legs, widened wrists/ankles, delayed dentition
  • [ ] Identify osteomalacia: bone pain (rib/spine/pelvis), proximal myopathy (waddling gait), Looser zones (pseudofractures)
  • [ ] State RDA: 600 IU (15 µg) 1–70y, 800 IU (20 µg) >70y; supplementation strategy: loading 50k IU weekly ×8w then maintenance 800–2000 IU/day
  • [ ] Recognise high-risk groups: dark skin, veiling, elderly, housebound, malabsorption, CKD, anticonvulsants, obesity, gastric bypass
  • [ ] Differentiate nutritional rickets from hypophosphataemic (XLH) and vitamin D-resistant rickets (VDDR type I/II)
  • [ ] Explain vitamin D toxicity: hypercalcaemia → nausea, polyuria, renal stones, metastatic calcification; threshold >10,000 IU/day chronic

2. 2. Definitions / Key Concepts

TermDefinition
Cholecalciferol (D3)Vitamin D3; synthesised in skin from 7-dehydrocholesterol by UVB (290–315 nm); also in oily fish, egg yolk, fortified foods
Ergocalciferol (D2)Vitamin D2; plant/fungal source (UV-irradiated ergosterol); less potent, shorter half-life than D3
7-DehydrocholesterolProvitamin D3 in epidermal keratinocytes; UVB → pre-vitamin D3 → thermal isomerisation → vitamin D3
Pre-vitamin D3Unstable intermediate; heat → vitamin D3 (t½ 2.5h at skin temp); excess UV → lumisterol/tachysterol (inactive)
25(OH)D (Calcidiol)Major circulating form; half-life 2–3 weeks; measured to assess status; stored in liver/fat; not biologically active
1,25(OH)2D (Calcitriol)Active hormone; half-life 4–6h; tightly regulated; acts via VDR (nuclear receptor) → gene transcription
VDR (Vitamin D Receptor)Nuclear receptor (RXR heterodimer); binds VDRE on DNA; mediates genomic effects (hours) + non-genomic (minutes)
CYP2R1Hepatic 25-hydroxylase; converts D3/D2 → 25(OH)D; not tightly regulated; genetic variants affect levels
CYP27B1Renal 1α-hydroxylase; converts 25(OH)D → 1,25(OH)2D; KEY regulated step
CYP24A124-hydroxylase; catabolises 25(OH)D and 1,25(OH)2D → inactive calcitroic acid; induced by 1,25(OH)2D, FGF23
PTHParathyroid hormone; ↑renal 1α-hydroxylase (CYP27B1); ↓renal phosphate reabsorption; ↑bone resorption
FGF23Fibroblast growth factor 23 (osteocytes); ↓1α-hydroxylase, ↑24-hydroxylase, ↓renal phosphate reabsorption
VDR PolymorphismsBsmI, FokI, TaqI, ApaI; associated with BMD, fracture risk, autoimmune disease, cancer susceptibility
Rachitic RosaryCostochondral junction enlargement (beading); palpable at anterior chest wall
Harrison's GrooveHorizontal depression at lower thoracic margin (diaphragm pull on softened ribs)
Looser Zones (Pseudofractures)Radiolucent lines perpendicular to cortex (medial femur, pubic rami, ribs, scapulae); pathognomonic osteomalacia
CraniotabesSoftening of skull bones (parietal/occipital); ping-pong ball feel; normal in preterm, pathological >3m if persistent
Hypophosphataemic Rickets (XLH)PHEX mutation → ↑FGF23 → renal phosphate wasting; normal Ca, normal/↑alk phos, low phosphate; refractory to vit D
VDDR Type ICYP27B1 mutation (1α-hydroxylase deficiency); autosomal recessive; responds to calcitriol
VDDR Type IIVDR mutation (end-organ resistance); autosomal recessive; alopecia (vs type I); high-dose calcitriol ± Ca infusion
Calcitriol AnaloguesAlfacalcidol (1α-OH-D3, rapid hepatic conversion), calcitriol (1,25(OH)2D), paricalcitol (selective VDR activator)

3. 3. Core Content

1. Section 1: Synthesis & Sources

Cutaneous synthesis (D3): 7-dehydrocholesterol (epidermis) + UVB (290–315 nm) → pre-vitamin D3 → thermal isomerisation (skin temp) → vitamin D3. Factors reducing synthesis: Melanin (SPF 15 = 99% block), sunscreen (SPF 30 = 97% block), latitude (>35° N/S winter), season (winter), time (10am–3pm peak), age (↓7-dehydrocholesterol 70% by 70y), clothing, glass (blocks UVB).

Dietary sources: D3 — oily fish (salmon 100g = 400–600 IU), cod liver oil (1 tsp = 450 IU), egg yolk (40 IU), fortified milk (100 IU/cup), fortified cereals; D2 — UV-irradiated mushrooms, fortified plant milks.

Bioavailability: D3 > D2 (D3 ↑25(OH)D 2–3x more effectively, longer half-life); fat-soluble → require dietary fat for absorption.

2. Section 2: Metabolic Pathway & Regulation

Step 1 (Liver): Vitamin D (D2/D3) → CYP2R1 (25-hydroxylase) → 25(OH)D (calcidiol). Minor CYP27A1 (mitochondrial). Not feedback-regulated. Half-life 15–30 days. Storage in adipose tissue → sequestration in obesity.

Step 2 (Kidney - proximal tubule): 25(OH)D + DBP (vitamin D binding protein) → megalin/cubilin endocytosis → CYP27B1 (1α-hydroxylase) → 1,25(OH)2D (calcitriol). Tightly regulated.

Regulators of CYP27B1 (1α-hydroxylase):

  • Stimulators (↑1,25(OH)2D): PTH (major), hypocalcaemia, hypophosphataemia, oestrogen, prolactin, growth hormone, calcitonin
  • Inhibitors (↓1,25(OH)2D): Hypercalcaemia, hyperphosphataemia, FGF23 (osteocyte), 1,25(OH)2D (feedback), ageing, CKD

Catabolism: CYP24A1 (24-hydroxylase) → 24,25(OH)2D → calcitroic acid (bile excretion); induced by 1,25(OH)2D, FGF23.

3. Section 3: Mechanism of Action

Genomic (VDR-RXR): 1,25(OH)2D + VDR → conformational change → heterodimer with RXR → binds VDRE (vitamin D response element) on DNA → recruits coactivators → transcription of target genes (hours–days). Targets: TRPV6 (Ca channel), calbindin-D9k (Ca buffer), PMCA1b (Ca pump), osteocalcin, RANKL, cathelicidin (antimicrobial).

Non-genomic (membrane VDR/PDIA3): Rapid (seconds–minutes) → PKC, MAPK, Ca²⁺ influx → intestinal Ca absorption, insulin secretion, muscle contraction.

4. Section 4: Calcium & Phosphate Homeostasis

Intestine: 1,25(OH)2D → ↑TRPV6 (apical Ca entry), ↑calbindin-D9k (cytosolic buffer), ↑PMCA1b (basolateral extrusion) → active transcellular Ca absorption (10–15% → 30–40%); paracellular (passive, vitamin D-independent). Phosphate: ↑NaPi-IIb (apical Na/P cotransporter).

Bone: 1,25(OH)2D → ↑osteoblast RANKL → osteoclast differentiation → bone resorption (↑Ca, ↑PO4); also osteoid mineralisation (if Ca×PO4 adequate).

Kidney: PTH + 1,25(OH)2D → ↓NaPi-IIa/IIc (proximal tubule) → phosphaturia; PTH → ↑Ca reabsorption (DCT); 1,25(OH)2D → ↑Ca reabsorption (weak).

Feedback: ↑Ca → CaSR (parathyroid) → ↓PTH → ↓1α-hydroxylase; ↑PO4 → ↑FGF23 → ↓1α-hydroxylase; ↑1,25(OH)2D → ↑CYP24A1 (self-limitation).

5. Section 5: Vitamin D Status & Definitions (Endocrine Society 2011)

25(OH)D LevelStatusClinical Correlate
<12 ng/mL (<30 nmol/L)DeficiencyRickets/osteomalacia, secondary HPT, ↑fracture
12–20 ng/mL (30–50 nmol/L)Insufficiency↑PTH, ↓Ca absorption, ↓BMD
20–30 ng/mL (50–75 nmol/L)AdequatePTH near normal, bone health acceptable
>30 ng/mL (>75 nmol/L)OptimalPTH minimised, maximal Ca absorption, fall prevention
>100 ng/mL (>250 nmol/L)Potential toxicityHypercalcaemia risk (rare <150 ng/mL)

PTH-threshold: PTH plateaus when 25(OH)D >30–40 ng/mL.

6. Section 6: Rickets (Children) — Clinical Features

Peak incidence: 6–24 months (rapid growth, low dietary intake, limited sun).

Skeletal: Craniotabes (soft skull >3m), frontal bossing, rachitic rosary (costochondral beading), Harrison's groove (diaphragmatic pull), widened wrists/ankles (metaphyseal flaring), bowed legs (genu varum ± valgum), kyphoscoliosis. X-ray: Cupping, fraying, widening of metaphyses; fraying of growth plate.

Non-skeletal: Hypocalcaemic tetany (carpopedal spasm, stridor, seizures), delayed dentition (enamel hypoplasia), delayed fontanelle closure, hypotonia, delayed milestones, sweating (head), irritability. Susceptibility to infections (pneumonia, diarrhoea).

Biochemistry: ↓25(OH)D, ↓1,25(OH)2D (late), ↓Ca (normal/low), ↓PO4, ↑ALP (bone isoform) (hallmark), ↑PTH (secondary HPT).

Radiographic healing: "Looser zones" appear at metaphyses during healing.

7. Section 7: Osteomalacia (Adults) — Clinical Features

Bone pain: Dull, aching, weight-bearing (lower back, pelvis, ribs, thighs); worse at night; often misdiagnosed as fibromyalgia.

Proximal myopathy: Symmetrical weakness (shoulder/hip girdle); waddling gait; difficulty rising from chair/climbing stairs (Gowers' sign); distinct from neuropathic weakness.

Looser zones (Pseudofractures): Radiolucent lines perpendicular to cortex; bilateral, symmetrical; sites: medial femoral neck, pubic/ischial rami, ribs (posterior), scapulae, ulna; pathognomonic.

Biochemistry: ↓25(OH)D (<12 ng/mL), ↓Ca (normal/low), ↓PO4, ↑ALP, ↑PTH (secondary HPT), normal/high 1,25(OH)2D (stimulated by PTH).

Differential: Paget's (↑ALP, ↑Ca normal, sclerotic), metastatic bone disease, myeloma, renal osteodystrophy, hypophosphatasia (↓ALP).

8. Section 8: High-Risk Groups & Causes

GroupMechanism
Dark skin (Fitzpatrick V/VI)Melanin = natural sunscreen (SPF 10–15); needs 6–10x sun exposure
Veiling / covered clothingBlocks UVB (e.g., burqa, niqab)
Elderly / housebound↓skin synthesis (7-dehydrocholesterol ↓70%), ↓sun exposure, ↓renal 1α-hydroxylase
MalabsorptionCoeliac, Crohn's, CF, biliary atresia, short bowel, bariatric (RYGB) — ↓fat absorption
CKD (stage 3+)↓renal 1α-hydroxylase, ↓DBP, ↑FGF23, ↑phosphate; requires active vit D analogue
AnticonvulsantsPhenytoin, carbamazepine, phenobarbital, valproate → ↑CYP24A1, ↑CYP3A4 → catabolism
Obesity (BMI >30)Sequestration in adipose tissue ↑volume of distribution; ↓bioavailability
Gastric bypass (RYGB)Bile + fat malabsorption; ↓vitamin D absorption
Strict vegan / milk allergyNo fortified dairy, no fish
GeneticCYP2R1 (↓25-hydroxylase), CYP27B1 (VDDR I), VDR (VDDR II), GC (DBP variants)

9. Section 9: Treatment & Supplementation

Deficiency treatment (Endocrine Society):

  • Children (1–18y): 2,000 IU/day D2/D3 ×6w OR 50,000 IU weekly ×6w → then maintenance 600–1,000 IU/day
  • Adults: 50,000 IU weekly ×8w (or 6,000 IU/day) → then maintenance 1,500–2,000 IU/day
  • Target: 25(OH)D >30 ng/mL (ideally 40–60 ng/mL)
  • D3 preferred over D2 (more potent, longer half-life)
  • Co-administer Ca: 1,000–1,200 mg/day elemental Ca (diet + supplement)
  • Monitor: 25(OH)D at 3–4 months; Ca, PO4, ALP, PTH

Special populations:

  • CKD: Use active analogues (alfacalcidol 0.25–1 µg/day, calcitriol 0.25–0.5 µg/day); monitor Ca×PO4 product
  • Malabsorption: Higher doses (10,000–50,000 IU/day) or IM D3 (300,000 IU q6m); consider UVB therapy
  • Obesity: 2–3x standard dose; target 25(OH)D >40 ng/mL
  • Anticonvulsants: 400–800 IU/day minimum; monitor 25(OH)D annually
  • Pregnancy: 600 IU/day (RDA); screen high-risk; >4,000 IU/day not routinely recommended

10. Section 10: Vitamin D-Resistant Rickets

DisorderGeneMechanismBiochemTreatment
Hypophosphataemic Rickets (XLH)PHEX↑FGF23 → renal phosphate wastingNormal Ca, ↓PO4, normal/↑ALP, normal 25(OH)D, ↑FGF23Phosphate + calcitriol (not vit D alone); burosumab (anti-FGF23 mAb)
VDDR Type ICYP27B11α-hydroxylase deficiency↓1,25(OH)2D, normal 25(OH)DCalcitriol 0.5–1 µg/day
VDDR Type IIVDREnd-organ resistance↑1,25(OH)2D, alopeciaHigh-dose calcitriol + high Ca infusion; VDR gene therapy
Hereditary Vit D Resistant Rickets (HVDRR)VDRSame as type IISameSame

11. Section 11: Vitamin D Toxicity

Threshold: Chronic >10,000 IU/day (250 µg) in adults; >2,000 IU/day in infants.

Mechanism: 25(OH)D saturates DBP → free 25(OH)D + 1,25(OH)2D enter cells → VDR overstimulation.

Features: Hypercalcaemia → nausea, vomiting, anorexia, polyuria, polydipsia, dehydration, renal stones (Ca oxalate), nephrocalcinosis, metastatic calcification (vessels, kidney, heart), pancreatitis, hypertension, confusion, coma.

Biochemistry: ↑25(OH)D (>150 ng/mL), ↑1,25(OH)2D, ↑Ca, ↑PO4, ↓PTH (suppressed), ↑ALP (if bone resorption).

Management: Stop vitamin D; IV saline hydration (3–4 L/day); loop diuretic (furosemide) after rehydration; bisphosphonates (pamidronate/zoledronic acid) for severe hypercalcaemia; corticosteroids (if granulomatous); dialysis if renal failure.

Not caused by: Sun exposure (pre-vitamin D3 → lumisterol/tachysterol inactivation); dietary overdose rare.

4. 4. Clinical Correlation

ScenarioActionNotes
18m child, bowed legs, rachitic rosary, ALP 800, 25(OH)D 8 ng/mLVit D3 2000 IU/day ×6w or 50k IU weekly ×6w + Ca 500 mg/day; orthopaedic reviewNutritional rickets; monitor ALP normalisation (6–12w)
65F, hijab-wearing, bone pain, waddling gait, Looser zones on pelvis X-ray, 25(OH)D 6 ng/mLD3 50k IU weekly ×8w → maintenance 2000 IU/day; Ca 1000 mg/day; fall preventionOsteomalacia; rule out malabsorption
70M, CKD stage 4, PTH 350, 25(OH)D 18, Ca 8.8, PO4 5.2Alfacalcidol 0.25 µg/day (or calcitriol); avoid cholecalciferol alone; monitor Ca×PO4CKD-MBD; active analogue needed; cinacalcet if PTH refractory
40F, phenytoin for epilepsy, 25(OH)D 14 ng/mLD3 4000 IU/day; monitor 25(OH)D annually; Ca/vit D supplementationEnzyme induction → catabolism
30M, BMI 42, gastric bypass 2y ago, 25(OH)D 10 ng/mLD3 50k IU weekly ×8w (or IM 300k IU q6m) + Ca citrate 1500 mg/dayMalabsorption + sequestration; needs 2–3x dose
80M, nursing home, fall, hip fracture, 25(OH)D 14 ng/mLD3 2000 IU/day + Ca 1000 mg/day; fall prevention programmeFall/fracture prevention; meta-analysis ↓falls 15–20%
55F, sarcoidosis, hypercalcaemia, 25(OH)D 20, 1,25(OH)2D 120Treat sarcoidosis (prednisolone); avoid vit D supplements; hydration, bisphosphanateGranulomatous 1α-hydroxylase dysregulation (macrophages)
10y girl, XLH (PHEX mutation), short stature, bowed legsPhosphate 30–50 mg/kg/day q4–6h + calcitriol 20–30 ng/kg/day; burosumab (anti-FGF23)XLH: phosphate wasting; do NOT use high-dose vit D (worsens nephrocalcinosis)

5. 5. High-Yield FCPS/MRCP Points

[!important]
- Must know: Two-step hydroxylation (liver 25-hydroxylase → kidney 1α-hydroxylase); regulators of 1α-hydroxylase (↑PTH, ↓Ca/PO4 stimulate; FGF23 inhibits); rickets signs (rosary, Harrison's, bowed legs); osteomalacia (bone pain, proximal myopathy, Looser zones); 25(OH)D thresholds (def <12, insuff 12–20, optimal >30 ng/mL); treatment loading 50k IU weekly ×8w; D3 > D2; high-risk groups; XLH = phosphate wasting + FGF23; CKD = active analogue
- Common viva: Why D3 > D2; regulation of 1α-hydroxylase; Looser zones vs rickets metaphyseal changes; XLH vs nutritional rickets biochem; why active vit D in CKD; hypercalcaemia in sarcoidosis (macrophage 1α-hydroxylase)
- Exam trap: Treating XLH with high-dose vit D (worsens nephrocalcinosis); using cholecalciferol in advanced CKD (no 1α-hydroxylase); missing secondary HPT in rickets; confusing Looser zones with metastatic lesions; vit D toxicity from sun (impossible)

6. 6. Common Confusions / Exam Traps

TrapCorrection
D2 = D3 potencyD3 2–3x more potent at raising 25(OH)D; longer half-life
Sun exposure → toxicityImpossible; pre-vit D3 → lumisterol/tachysterol (inactive) with excess UV
1,25(OH)2D best status markerNO; short half-life, tightly regulated; 25(OH)D is storage form
XLH = vit D deficiencyXLH = FGF23-mediated phosphate wasting; normal 25(OH)D; refractory to vit D
CKD → give cholecalciferolCKD 3b+ needs active analogue (alfacalcidol/calcitriol); no renal 1α-hydroxylase
Hypercalcaemia in sarcoidosis = vit D excessMacrophage 1α-hydroxylase unregulated; 1,25(OH)2D ↑, 25(OH)D normal/low
Looser zones = metastatic lytic lesionsLooser zones: perpendicular to cortex, bilateral, symmetrical (pubic rami, femoral neck)
VDDR I vs IIType I: CYP27B1 defect → low 1,25(OH)2D → responds to calcitriol; Type II: VDR defect → high 1,25(OH)2D, alopecia
Obesity = just need higher doseSequestration in fat + volumetric dilution; needs 2–3x dose + target >40 ng/mL
Rickets = only bonesAlso: hypocalcaemic tetany, cardiomyopathy, delayed dentition, myopathy, infections

7. 7. Mnemonics

  • Synthesis: SKIN UVB → 7-DHC → PRE-D3 → HEAT → D3 (melanin/sunscreen/latitude/age ↓)
  • Two-step hydroxylation: LIVER 25-OH → KIDNEY 1α-OH → ACTIVE (CYP2R1 → CYP27B1)
  • 1α-hydroxylase regulators: PTH HYPE = PTH ↑, Hypocalcaemia, Y (hypoPhosphataemia), Prolactin, Estrogen; Inhibit: FGF23, Ca, PO4, 1,25-D
  • Rickets signs: CRAB = Craniotabes, Rachitic rosary, A (Harrison's groove → Anchoring diaphragm), Bowed legs
  • Osteomalacia triad: BONE = Bone pain, Osteomalacia (Looser zones), Non-traumatic fractures, E (proximal myopathy → waddling gait)
  • Looser zones sites: FISH = Femur (medial neck), Ischial/pubic rami, Scapula, Humerus/ribs
  • XLH: PHEX mutation → FGF23 ↑ → phosphate wasting (normal Ca, normal vit D) → burosumab (anti-FGF23)
  • VDDR: Type I = CYP27B1 (1α-hydroxylase) → low 1,25-D → calcitriol works; Type II = VDR → high 1,25-D + alopecia
  • CKD vit D: NO 1α-OH in kidney → give alfacalcidol/calcitriol (bypass renal step)
  • Sarcoidosis hyperCa: MACROPHAGE 1α-OH unregulated → ↑1,25-D → hyperCa; treat underlying, avoid vit D
  • Toxicity: HYPERCALCAEMIA → stones, calcification, polyuria; >10k IU/day chronic

8. 8. Mind Map

mindmap
  root((Vitamin D))
    Forms
      D3 (Cholecalciferol)
        Skin UVB → 7-DHC → Pre-D3 → D3
        Animal sources: fish, egg, liver
      D2 (Ergocalciferol)
        Fungi/yeast UV → Ergosterol → D2
    Metabolism
      Step 1: Liver
        CYP2R1 (25-hydroxylase)
        D2/D3 → 25(OH)D (Calcidiol)
        Half-life 2–3 weeks
        Storage form
      Step 2: Kidney
        CYP27B1 (1α-hydroxylase)
        25(OH)D → 1,25(OH)2D (Calcitriol)
        Half-life 4–6h
        Active hormone
        TIGHTLY REGULATED
      Regulators (1α-OH)
        Stimulate: PTH, ↓Ca, ↓PO4, GH, Prolactin, Estrogen
        Inhibit: FGF23, ↑Ca, ↑PO4, 1,25-D feedback, Ageing, CKD
    Mechanism
      Genomic: VDR + RXR → VDRE → Transcription (TRPV6, Calbindin, Osteocalcin)
      Non-genomic: Membrane VDR → PKC, MAPK, Rapid Ca influx
    Calcium/Phosphate
      Intestine: TRPV6, Calbindin-D9k, PMCA1b (active transcellular)
      Bone: RANKL → Osteoclast resorption; Osteoid mineralisation
      Kidney: PTH + 1,25-D → ↓NaPi (phosphaturia); PTH → ↑Ca reabs (DCT)
    Status (Endocrine Society)
      Deficiency: <12 ng/mL
      Insufficiency: 12–20 ng/mL
      Adequate: 20–30 ng/mL
      Optimal: >30 ng/mL
      Toxicity risk: >100 ng/mL
    Deficiency
      Children: RICKETS
        Craniotabes, Rosary, Harrison's, Bowed legs
        Hypocalcaemic tetany, Delayed dentition, ↑ALP, ↑PTH
      Adults: OSTEOMALACIA
        Bone pain, Proximal myopathy (waddling)
        Looser zones (Pseudofractures)
        ↑ALP, ↑PTH, Normal/low Ca, ↓PO4
    Risk Groups
      Dark skin, Veiling, Elderly, Housebound
      Malabsorption (Coeliac, CF, Bariatric)
      CKD, Anticonvulsants, Obesity, Vegan
      Genetic (CYP2R1, CYP27B1, VDR, GC)
    Treatment
      Deficiency: 50k IU weekly ×8w → Maintenance 1500–2000 IU/day
      D3 preferred (2–3x potency)
      Target 25(OH)D >30 ng/mL
      Special: CKD (Alfacalcidol/Calcitriol), Obesity (2–3x), Malabsorption (IM/High dose)
    Resistant Rickets
      XLH (PHEX → FGF23 → Phosphate wasting) → Phosphate + Calcitriol / BUROSUMAB
      VDDR I (CYP27B1) → Calcitriol
      VDDR II (VDR + Alopecia) → High-dose Calcitriol + Ca
    Toxicity
      >10,000 IU/day chronic
      Hypercalcaemia → Nausea, Stones, Nephrocalcinosis, Metastatic calcification
      Stop D, Hydration, Furosemide, Bisphosphonate

9. 9. -Hour Recall Prompts

  1. Draw 2-step hydroxylation: Liver CYP2R1 → 25(OH)D; Kidney CYP27B1 → 1,25(OH)2D; regulators
  2. List 5 rickets signs (CRAB) + x-ray findings (cupping, fraying, widening)
  3. Osteomalacia triad: bone pain, proximal myopathy, Looser zones (sites: FISH)
  4. 25(OH)D thresholds: deficiency <12, optimal >30 ng/mL; PTH plateaus at 30–40
  5. XLH vs nutritional rickets: PHEX/FGF23 vs vit D deficiency; phosphate wasting vs low Ca
  6. CKD vit D: why active analogue (alfacalcidol/calcitriol)? No renal 1α-hydroxylase
  7. Treatment loading: 50k IU weekly ×8w → maintenance 1500–2000 IU/day; D3 preferred
  8. Sarcoidosis hypercalcaemia mechanism: macrophage 1α-hydroxylase unregulated

11. 11. Must Know / Should Know / Nice to Know

PriorityContent
Must Know 🔴Two-step hydroxylation (CYP2R1/CYP27B1); regulators of 1α-hydroxylase (PTH, Ca, PO4, FGF23); rickets/osteomalacia features; 25(OH)D thresholds; treatment loading/maintenance; D3 > D2; XLH (PHEX/FGF23); CKD needs active analogue; Looser zones
Should Know 🟡VDR polymorphisms; non-genomic actions; XLH management (burosumab); VDDR I vs II; obesity dosing; anticonvulsant catabolism; granulomatous disease hypercalcaemia
Nice to Know 🟢CYP24A1 catabolism; genetic rickets (hypophosphatasia ↓ALP); vitamin D in immune/autoimmunity/cancer; calcitriol analogues (paricalcitol, doxercalciferol); IM D3 dosing

12. 12. My Weak Points

  • [ ] Exact VDR-RXR-VDRE transcriptional complex
  • [ ] FGF23-Klotho signalling details
  • [ ] Burosumab dosing/monitoring in XLH

13. 13. Self-Test Scorecard

DomainScore /10Target /10

14. 14. Exam Answer Modes

1. Long Answer / Essay (20 min)

Topic: "Vitamin D metabolism, deficiency states, and management"

  • Synthesis: cutaneous D3 (UVB + 7-DHC), dietary D2/D3
  • Two-step hydroxylation: liver CYP2R1 → 25(OH)D (storage); kidney CYP27B1 → 1,25(OH)2D (active hormone)
  • Regulation: ↑PTH, ↓Ca, ↓PO4 stimulate 1α-hydroxylase; FGF23, ↑Ca, ↑PO4, 1,25-D feedback inhibit
  • Mechanism: genomic (VDR-RXR-VDRE → TRPV6, calbindin, osteocalcin) + non-genomic
  • Deficiency: children = rickets (craniotabes, rosary, Harrison's, bowed legs, tetany); adults = osteomalacia (bone pain, proximal myopathy, Looser zones); biochem: ↓25(OH)D, ↑ALP, ↑PTH
  • Status: def <12, insuff 12–20, optimal >30 ng/mL
  • High-risk: dark skin, veiling, elderly, malabsorption, CKD, anticonvulsants, obesity
  • Treatment: loading 50k IU weekly ×8w → maintenance 1500–2000 IU/day D3; target >30 ng/mL
  • Special: CKD (alfacalcidol/calcitriol), XLH (phosphate + calcitriol/burosumab), VDDR I/II
  • Toxicity: hypercalcaemia >10k IU/day chronic; sun exposure never toxic

2. Short Note (7 min)

Topic: "Rickets vs Osteomalacia"

  • Rickets (children): Growth plate defect → craniotabes, rosary, Harrison's, bowed legs, widened wrists; hypocalcaemic tetany; X-ray: cupping/fraying/widening metaphyses.
  • Osteomalacia (adults): Bone mineralisation defect → bone pain, proximal myopathy (waddling), Looser zones (pseudofractures: femoral neck, pubic rami, ribs); ↑ALP, ↑PTH.
  • Common: ↓25(OH)D, ↓Ca/PO4 (late), ↑ALP, ↑PTH.
  • Biochem: Rickets = hypocalcaemia prominent; Osteomalacia = hypophosphataemia often more marked.

3. Viva Answer (3 min)

Q: "Why is cholecalciferol ineffective in stage 4 CKD?"

"A: CKD → loss of renal 1α-hydroxylase (CYP27B1). Cholecalciferol requires renal conversion to active 1,25(OH)2D. In CKD, give active analogue (alfacalcidol 1α-OH-D3 → hepatic 25-hydroxylase → calcitriol; or calcitriol directly) which bypasses the renal step. Monitor Ca×PO4 product."

4. Ward Case Discussion (5 min)

Case: 25M, XLH (PHEX mutation), short stature, genu valgum, phosphatases normal, 25(OH)D 35 ng/mL.

"Diagnosis: X-linked hypophosphataemic rickets (PHEX mutation → ↑FGF23 → renal phosphate wasting). Do NOT give high-dose vitamin D (worsens nephrocalcinosis). Treatment: Phosphate 30–50 mg/kg/day divided q4–6h + calcitriol 20–30 ng/kg/day. Monitor: Ca, PO4, ALP, renal ultrasound (nephrocalcinosis), PTH. New: Burosumab (anti-FGF23 mAb) monthly SC — normalises phosphate, improves rickets."

5. Last-Night-Before-Exam Sheet (1 min)

  • Synthesis: UVB + 7-DHC → D3; diet D2/D3
  • Metabolism: Liver CYP2R1 → 25(OH)D (storage, t½ 2–3w) → Kidney CYP27B1 → 1,25(OH)2D (active, t½ 4–6h)
  • Regulation: 1α-OH stimulated by PTH, ↓Ca, ↓PO4; inhibited by FGF23, ↑Ca, ↑PO4
  • Status: Def <12, Insuff 12–20, Optimal >30 ng/mL
  • Rickets (kids): CRAB (Craniotabes, Rosary, Harrison's, Bowed legs) + tetany + ↑ALP
  • Osteomalacia (adults): Bone pain, proximal myopathy, Looser zones (FISH sites), ↑ALP
  • XLH: PHEX → FGF23 ↑ → phosphate wasting; normal Ca/25(OH)D; Rx: phosphate + calcitriol / burosumab
  • CKD: Alfacalcidol/calcitriol (bypass renal 1α-OH)
  • Treatment: 50k IU weekly ×8w → 1500–2000 IU/day D3; target >30 ng/mL
  • Toxicity: Hypercalcaemia >10k IU/day; stones, calcification

15. 15. MCQs (10)

  1. Rate-limiting step in vitamin D activation:

A. Hepatic 25-hydroxylase (CYP2R1)

B. Renal 1α-hydroxylase (CYP27B1)

C. Cutaneous UVB conversion

D. DBP binding

  1. Active form of vitamin D:

A. Cholecalciferol

B. 25(OH)D

C. 1,25(OH)2D (Calcitriol)

D. 24,25(OH)2D

  1. Major stimulator of renal 1α-hydroxylase:

A. FGF23

B. Hypercalcaemia

C. PTH

D. Hyperphosphataemia

  1. Rickets X-ray finding at metaphysis:

A. Sclerosis

B. Cupping, fraying, widening

C. Periosteal reaction

D. Osteolytic lesions

  1. Pathognomonic radiographic sign of osteomalacia:

A. Osteophytes

B. Looser zones (pseudofractures)

C. Vertebral compression fractures

D. Cortical thickening

  1. 25(OH)D level defining deficiency (Endocrine Society):

A. <20 ng/mL

B. <12 ng/mL

C. <30 ng/mL

D. <5 ng/mL

  1. XLH (Hypophosphataemic rickets) biochemical profile:

A. Low Ca, low PO4, low 25(OH)D

B. Normal Ca, low PO4, normal 25(OH)D, ↑FGF23

C. Low Ca, normal PO4, low 25(OH)D

D. High Ca, low PO4, high 25(OH)D

  1. Vitamin D preparation for CKD stage 4:

A. Cholecalciferol 50,000 IU weekly

B. Alfacalcidol (1α-OH-D3) or Calcitriol

C. Ergocalciferol 50,000 IU weekly

D. Sunlight exposure only

  1. Vitamin D toxicity threshold (chronic adult):

A. >2,000 IU/day

B. >4,000 IU/day

C. >10,000 IU/day

D. >50,000 IU/day

  1. Sarcoidosis hypercalcaemia mechanism:

A. Excess vitamin D intake

B. Macrophage 1α-hydroxylase unregulated (↑1,25(OH)2D)

C. PTH excess

D. Bone metastases

16. 16. SBA Questions (5)

  1. A 2-year-old child presents with bowed legs, rachitic rosary, and ALP 950 U/L. 25(OH)D 9 ng/mL. Best initial treatment?

A. Calcium 1000 mg/day alone

B. Vitamin D3 2000 IU/day (or 50,000 IU weekly) ×6 weeks + Calcium 500 mg/day

C. Calcitriol 0.25 µg/day

D. Single dose vitamin D 600,000 IU IM

E. High-phosphorus diet

  1. A 60-year-old woman with hijab, bone pain, proximal myopathy, Looser zones on pubic rami. 25(OH)D 6 ng/mL. Management?

A. Calcium 1200 mg/day alone

B. Cholecalciferol 1000 IU/day

C. Cholecalciferol 50,000 IU weekly ×8 weeks → maintenance 2000 IU/day + Calcium 1000 mg/day

D. Calcitriol 0.5 µg/day lifelong

E. Teriparatide SC daily

  1. A 10-year-old girl with short stature, genu valgum, normal calcium, low phosphate, normal ALP, 25(OH)D 32 ng/mL. Father affected. Genetic basis?

A. CYP27B1 mutation

B. PHEX mutation (XLH)

C. VDR mutation

D. Sodium-phosphate cotransporter mutation

E. FGF23 mutation (AD hypophosphataemic rickets)

  1. A 65-year-old man with CKD stage 4 (eGFR 22), secondary hyperparathyroidism (PTH 320), 25(OH)D 18 ng/mL. Best vitamin D therapy?

A. Cholecalciferol 50,000 IU weekly

B. Alfacalcidol 0.25 µg daily (or calcitriol 0.25 µg)

C. Ergocalciferol 50,000 IU weekly

D. No vitamin D needed (PTH already high)

E. Calcitriol only if Ca low

  1. A 40-year-old woman with sarcoidosis presents with hypercalcaemia (Ca 12.2 mg/dL), normal PTH, elevated 1,25(OH)2D, normal 25(OH)D. Cause?

A. Exogenous vitamin D toxicity

B. Primary hyperparathyroidism

C. Granulomatous macrophage 1α-hydroxylase dysregulation

D. PTHrP secretion

E. Milk-alkali syndrome

17. 17. Flashcards

  • Q: Two hydroxylation steps

A: Liver CYP2R1: D2/D3 → 25(OH)D; Kidney CYP27B1: 25(OH)D → 1,25(OH)2D

  • Q: 25(OH)D vs 1,25(OH)2D

A: 25(OH)D = storage (t½ 2–3w); 1,25(OH)2D = active hormone (t½ 4–6h)

  • Q: 1α-hydroxylase stimulators/inhibitors

A: Stimulate: PTH, ↓Ca, ↓PO4; Inhibit: FGF23, ↑Ca, ↑PO4, 1,25-D feedback

  • Q: Rickets signs (CRAB)

A: Craniotabes, Rachitic rosary, Harrison's groove, Bowed legs

  • Q: Osteomalacia triad

A: Bone pain, Proximal myopathy, Looser zones (FISH sites)

  • Q: Looser zones locations

A: Femur (medial neck), Ischial/pubic rami, Scapula, Humerus/ribs

  • Q: 25(OH)D thresholds

A: Deficiency <12, Insufficiency 12–20, Optimal >30 ng/mL

  • Q: XLH genetics/biochem

A: PHEX mutation → ↑FGF23 → renal phosphate wasting; normal Ca, normal 25(OH)D, ↑FGF23

  • Q: XLH treatment

A: Phosphate + calcitriol; Burosumab (anti-FGF23 mAb); NO high-dose vit D

  • Q: VDDR I vs II

A: Type I: CYP27B1 defect → low 1,25-D → calcitriol works; Type II: VDR defect → high 1,25-D + alopecia

  • Q: CKD vit D

A: No renal 1α-OH → give active analogue (Alfacalcidol/Calcitriol)

  • Q: Treatment loading

A: 50k IU weekly ×8w → maintenance 1500–2000 IU/day D3; target 25(OH)D >30 ng/mL

  • Q: Toxicity threshold

A: Chronic >10,000 IU/day → hypercalcaemia, stones, calcification

  • Q: Sarcoidosis hyperCa

A: Macrophage 1α-OH unregulated → ↑1,25-D; treat sarcoid, avoid vit D

  • Q: D3 vs D2

A: D3 2–3x more potent, longer t½; D2 less effective at raising 25(OH)D

18. 18. Answer Key with Explanations

1. MCQs

  1. B — CYP27B1 (1α-hydroxylase) in kidney is the regulated, rate-limiting step; hepatic CYP2R1 is not feedback-inhibited.
  2. C — 1,25(OH)2D (calcitriol) is the biologically active hormone; 25(OH)D is storage form.
  3. C — PTH is the major physiological stimulator; FGF23, hypercalcaemia, hyperphosphataemia inhibit.
  4. B — Rachitic metaphysis: cupping (concave), fraying (irregular), widening (splayed).
  5. B — Looser zones (pseudofractures) are radiolucent lines perpendicular to cortex; pathognomonic for osteomalacia.
  6. B — Endocrine Society: deficiency <12 ng/mL (<30 nmol/L); insufficiency 12–20 ng/mL.
  7. B — XLH: PHEX mutation → ↑FGF23 → renal phosphate wasting; normal Ca, normal 25(OH)D, ↓PO4, ↑FGF23.
  8. B — CKD 3b+: loss of renal 1α-hydroxylase → must give active analogue (alfacalcidol/calcitriol).
  9. C — Chronic intake >10,000 IU/day in adults risks hypercalcaemia/toxicity.
  10. B — Sarcoid macrophages express unregulated CYP27B1 → ↑1,25(OH)2D → hypercalcaemia; 25(OH)D normal.

2. SBAs

  1. B — Nutritional rickets: D3 loading (2000 IU/day or 50k IU weekly ×6w) + Ca; calcitriol not first-line.
  2. C — Osteomalacia: loading 50k IU weekly ×8w → maintenance 2000 IU/day + Ca; not calcitriol unless CKD.
  3. B — XLH: PHEX mutation (X-linked dominant); normal Ca/25(OH)D, low PO4, ↑FGF23; father affected = XLH.
  4. B — CKD 4: no renal 1α-hydroxylase; alfacalcidol (1α-OH-D3) hepatic 25-hydroxylation → calcitriol; or direct calcitriol.
  5. C — Granulomatous disease: macrophage CYP27B1 unregulated by Ca/PTH → ↑1,25-D → hypercalcaemia.

19. 19. Summary

Vitamin D is a Must Know 🔴 topic for FCPS/MRCP.

Key takeaway: Vitamin D (D3 skin UVB, D2 diet) → liver 25-hydroxylase (CYP2R1) → 25(OH)D (storage) → kidney 1α-hydroxylase (CYP27B1) → 1,25(OH)2D (active hormone). Regulation: ↑PTH, ↓Ca, ↓PO4 stimulate; FGF23, ↑Ca, ↑PO4 inhibit. Deficiency: children = rickets (craniotabes, rosary, Harrison's, bowed legs, tetany); adults = osteomalacia (bone pain, proximal myopathy, Looser zones). Status: <12 ng/mL deficiency, >30 ng/mL optimal. High-risk: dark skin, veiling, elderly, malabsorption, CKD, anticonvulsants, obesity. Treatment: D3 50k IU weekly ×8w → 1500–2000 IU/day maintenance. Special: CKD needs active analogue (alfacalcidol/calcitriol); XLH (PHEX/FGF23) = phosphate wasting, treat with phosphate+calcitriol/burosumab. Toxicity: >10k IU/day chronic → hypercalcaemia.

Exam focus: Two-step hydroxylation, regulators, rickets/osteomalacia features, 25(OH)D thresholds, XLH biochem, CKD management.

Clinical relevance: Universal supplementation in at-risk groups; fall/fracture prevention in elderly; XLH novel therapy burosumab.

topics.title: Vitamin E Antioxidant Function Deficiency
topics.html:
Related: [[Nutritional Factors in Disease MOC]], [[Davidson Chapter 22 - Nutritional Factors in Disease Hierarchy]], [[../00_Index/Medicine MOC|Medicine MOC]]

[!important]
Vitamin E = 8 isoforms (4 tocopherols α/β/γ/δ, 4 tocotrienols); α-tocopherol most biologically active; lipid-soluble antioxidant stops lipid peroxidation chain; deficiency RARE (fat malabsorption) → spinocerebellar ataxia, neuropathy, retinopathy; high-dose (>400 IU/day) ↑mortality & haemorrhagic stroke.

1. 1. Learning Objectives

  • [ ] List 8 vitamin E isoforms (α/β/γ/δ-tocopherols, α/β/γ/δ-tocotrienols); α-tocopherol preferential retention via α-TTP
  • [ ] Explain antioxidant mechanism: LOO• + α-TOH → LOOH + α-TO• (tocopheroxyl radical) → recycled by vitamin C/GSH
  • [ ] State RDA: 15 mg (22.4 IU) α-tocopherol/day adult; UL 1000 mg (1500 IU); natural RRR-α-tocopherol 2x bioavailable vs synthetic all-rac
  • [ ] Identify deficiency causes: fat malabsorption (CF, cholestasis, abetalipoproteinaemia, isolated vit E deficiency, short bowel)
  • [ ] Recognise deficiency triad: spinocerebellar ataxia, peripheral neuropathy, pigmented retinopathy (+ haemolytic anaemia in preterms)
  • [ ] Describe abetalipoproteinaemia (MTTP mutation → no apoB → no chylomicron/VLDL) and isolated vit E deficiency (TTPA mutation → ↓α-TTP)
  • [ ] State high-dose risks: meta-analysis ↑all-cause mortality >400 IU/day; SELECT trial ↑prostate cancer; ↑haemorrhagic stroke
  • [ ] Know drug interactions: warfarin (↑INR), statins (↓HDL benefit), chemotherapy (↓efficacy), antiplatelets (↑bleeding)

2. 2. Definitions / Key Concepts

TermDefinition
TocopherolsSaturated side chain (phytyl); α, β, γ, δ differ in methyl groups on chromanol ring
TocotrienolsUnsaturated side chain (3 double bonds); α, β, γ, δ; more potent antioxidant (membrane mobility)
α-TTP (α-Tocopherol Transfer Protein)Hepatic protein; binds α-tocopherol preferentially (vs γ/δ) → incorporation into VLDL → plasma; TTPA gene mutation → isolated vit E deficiency
RRR-α-TocopherolNatural stereoisomer (2R,4'R,8'R); "d-α-tocopherol"; 100% bioactivity; 1 mg = 1.49 IU
All-rac-α-TocopherolSynthetic mixture of 8 stereoisomers; "dl-α-tocopherol"; ~50% bioactivity; 1 mg = 2.22 IU (but only 1/8 is RRR)
IU Conversion1 mg RRR-α-tocopherol = 1.49 IU; 1 mg all-rac = 2.22 IU (label); but effective bioactivity ratio ~2:1
Lipid PeroxidationFree radical chain: Initiation (LH → L•) → Propagation (L• + O₂ → LOO• → LOOH + L•) → Termination
Antioxidant CycleLOO• + α-TOH → LOOH + α-TO• (tocopheroxyl radical); α-TO• + Ascorbate/GSH → α-TOH (recycled)
α-TocopherolMajor membrane antioxidant; 1 molecule/200–500 phospholipids; protects PUFA in lipoprotein/membrane
γ-TocopherolMajor form in US diet (soybean/corn oil); traps reactive nitrogen species (RNS); ↑COX-2 inhibition
AbetalipoproteinaemiaMTTP mutation (AR) → no microsomal triglyceride transfer protein → no chylomicron/VLDL assembly → fat malabsorption + vit A/D/E/K deficiency; acanthocytosis, retinitis pigmentosa, neuropathy
Isolated Vit E Deficiency (AVED)TTPA mutation (AR, chromosome 8) → ↓α-TTP → α-tocopherol not secreted into VLDL → plasma vit E <5% normal; onset 5–15y; responds to high-dose oral vit E (800–1200 IU/day)
Preterm Haemolytic AnaemiaLow vit E stores (transplacental transfer late 3rd trimester) + high O₂ + iron → lipid peroxidation of RBC membrane → haemolysis; prevented by vit E supplementation
SELECT TrialSelenium + Vitamin E Cancer Prevention Trial; 35,000 men; vit E 400 IU/day + Se 200 µg → ↑prostate cancer HR 1.17 (significant); stopped early
Meta-Analysis (Miller 2005)19 trials, 136,000 pts; vit E ≥400 IU/day → ↑all-cause mortality (RR 1.04, p=0.04); dose-response >150 IU/day
HDL & StatinsHDL-C ↑ with niacin + statin; adding vit E (along with vit C, β-carotene) → blunts HDL₂ rise (ARBITER, HATS)

3. 3. Core Content

1. Section 1: Chemistry, Isoforms & Bioavailability

8 natural isoforms: 4 tocopherols (saturated phytyl tail) + 4 tocotrienols (unsaturated isoprenoid tail with 3 double bonds). Each has α, β, γ, δ forms based on methyl substitution on chromanol ring (α = 3 methyls, β/γ = 2, δ = 1).

Biological potency: α-tocopherol > β-tocopherol > γ-tocopherol > δ-tocopherol (α-TTP affinity). Tocotrienols > tocopherols antioxidant capacity (unsaturated tail → better membrane penetration).

Absorption: Like other fat-soluble vitamins — mixed micelles → enterocyte → chylomicron secretion → lymph. α-TTP in liver selectively transfers α-tocopherol to VLDL for plasma distribution; γ/δ-tocopherols metabolised by CYP4F2 → excreted in bile/urine.

Plasma levels: α-tocopherol 5–20 µg/mL (11.6–46 µmol/L); γ-tocopherol 1–5 µg/mL (varies by diet).

Bioavailability: Natural RRR-α-tocopherol (d-α-tocopherol) 2x synthetic all-rac-α-tocopherol (dl-α-tocopherol). Ester forms (acetate, succinate) hydrolysed in gut → free tocopherol.

2. Section 2: Antioxidant Mechanism

Lipid peroxidation chain reaction:

  1. Initiation: ROS (OH•, ROO•) abstract H from lipid (LH) → lipid radical (L•)
  2. Propagation: L• + O₂ → LOO• (lipid peroxyl radical) → LOO• + LH → LOOH (lipid hydroperoxide) + L• (chain continues)
  3. Termination: Radical-radical reactions or antioxidant intervention

Vitamin E action (chain-breaking):

  • LOO• + α-TOH → LOOH + α-TO• (tocopheroxyl radical)
  • α-TO• relatively unreactive (resonance-stabilised) → chain terminated
  • Recycling: α-TO• + Ascorbate (vit C) → α-TOH + Ascorbyl radical → GSH/NADPH → Ascorbate
  • Synergy: Vit E (lipid phase) + Vit C (aqueous phase) + GSH (intracellular) = antioxidant network

Non-antioxidant functions: Gene expression (↓CD36 scavenger receptor, ↓VCAM-1), inhibition of PKC (smooth muscle proliferation), platelet aggregation inhibition (↓TXA₂, ↑prostacyclin), immune modulation (↑IL-2, NK activity), regulation of HMG-CoA reductase.

3. Section 3: Requirements & Sources

RDA (IOM 2000): 15 mg/day (22.4 IU) α-tocopherol for adults; based on plasma α-tocopherol 12 µmol/L at intake 12 mg/day.

UL: 1,000 mg/day (1,500 IU natural / 1,100 IU synthetic) — based on bleeding risk.

Dietary sources (α-tocopherol mg/serving):

  • Wheat germ oil (1 Tbsp = 20 mg), Sunflower oil (1 Tbsp = 5.6 mg), Almonds (1 oz = 7.3 mg), Sunflower seeds (1 oz = 7.4 mg), Peanut butter (2 Tbsp = 2.9 mg), Spinach (½ cup cooked = 1.9 mg), Avocado (½ = 2.1 mg)
  • γ-Tocopherol dominant in US diet: Soybean oil, corn oil, canola oil (major contributors)

Deficiency risk: Extremely rare in healthy adults (large body stores, efficient recycling). Only in fat malabsorption or genetic defects.

4. Section 4: Deficiency — Causes & Clinical Features

CauseMechanism
Cystic fibrosisPancreatic insufficiency → fat malabsorption
Cholestatic liver diseaseBiliary atresia, PSC, Alagille → ↓bile salts → fat malabsorption
Abetalipoproteinaemia (Bassen-Kornzweig)MTTP mutation → no apoB → no chylomicron/VLDL → fat malabsorption + all fat-soluble vit deficiency
Isolated Vitamin E Deficiency (AVED)TTPA mutation → ↓α-TTP → α-toc not secreted in VLDL; plasma α-toc <5% normal
Short bowel / intestinal resection↓Surface area, ↓bile salt pool
CRF / dialysisLosses, dietary restriction, oxidative stress
Genetic (CYP4F2, SCARB1)Rare variants affecting metabolism/transport

Clinical Triad (Neurological):

  1. Spinocerebellar ataxia — gait instability, dysmetria, intention tremor, dysarthria, nystagmus; posterior column + cerebellar degeneration
  2. Peripheral neuropathy — axonal sensorimotor; loss of vibration/position sense; areflexia; distal weakness
  3. Pigmented retinopathy — granular retinal pigment epithelium changes; night blindness; "retinitis pigmentosa-like"

Additional: Haemolytic anaemia (preterm infants), immune impairment, muscle weakness, cognitive decline (late).

Abetalipoproteinaemia (MTTP, AR): Onset infancy — steatorrhoea, failure to thrive, acanthocytosis (spur cells), retinitis pigmentosa, spinocerebellar degeneration, coagulopathy (vit K deficiency). Landmark: Absent apoB, LDL, VLDL; cholesterol <50 mg/dL; triglycerides <20 mg/dL. Treatment: High-dose fat-soluble vitamins (vit E 100–200 IU/kg/day), medium-chain triglycerides, fat restriction.

AVED (TTPA, AR): Onset 5–15 years — progressive ataxia, areflexia, proprioception loss, retinopathy; plasma α-toc <5% normal with normal lipids/lipoproteins. Treatment: High-dose oral α-tocopherol 800–1200 IU/day (RRR) → halts progression. Genetic testing confirmatory.

Preterm Infants: Low vit E stores (transplacental transfer peaks 32–36w), high O₂ exposure, iron catalysis → lipid peroxidation of RBC membranes → haemolytic anaemia. Prevention: Vit E 10–20 IU/kg/day in TPN/formula (modern formulas supplemented).

5. Section 5: Toxicity & High-Dose Risks

UL: 1000 mg/day (1500 IU natural, 1100 IU synthetic) — based on bleeding tendency (antiplatelet effect).

Acute toxicity: Rare; very high doses (>10,000 IU) → nausea, diarrhoea, fatigue, weakness, blurred vision.

Chronic high-dose risks (Epidemiological):

  • All-cause mortality ↑: Miller et al. 2005 meta-analysis (19 RCTs, 136,000 pts): vit E ≥400 IU/day → ↑all-cause mortality (RR 1.04, 95% CI 1.01–1.07); dose-response >150 IU/day.
  • Haemorrhagic stroke ↑: HPFS/WHS: vit E 600 IU q2d → ↑haemorrhagic stroke (RR 1.22); no benefit for ischaemic stroke.
  • Prostate cancer ↑: SELECT trial (35,533 men): vit E 400 IU/day + Se 200 µg/day → ↑prostate cancer HR 1.17 (99% CI 1.004–1.36); vit E alone also ↑risk; stopped early 2008.
  • Heart failure ↑: HOPE-TOO, GISSI-Prevenzione: vit E 400 IU/day → ↑HF hospitalisation.
  • All-cause mortality meta-analysis: Cochrane 2018 (78 RCTs, 299,000): vit E ↑mortality (RR 1.03); no cardiovascular benefit.

Mechanism of harm: High-dose α-tocopherol → ↓γ-tocopherol (competition for α-TTP) → loss of γ-tocopherol's unique RNS trapping & anti-inflammatory effects; pro-oxidant at high concentrations (tocopheroxyl radical not recycled); interference with vitamin K (↑bleeding); modulation of CYP450, signal transduction.

6. Section 6: Drug Interactions

DrugInteractionClinical Significance
Warfarin↑INR (synergistic anticoagulation)Monitor INR; vit E inhibits vitamin K-dependent carboxylase?; case reports of bleeding
Statins↓HDL benefit (blunts HDL₂ rise)ARBITER/HATS: vit E + vit C + β-carotene blunted niacin+statin HDL rise
Antiplatelets (ASA, clopidogrel)↑Bleeding riskInhibits platelet aggregation (↓TXA₂, ↑PGI₂); caution in high-dose
Chemotherapy↓Efficacy (antioxidant protects tumour)Theoretical concern; avoid high-dose during chemo/radiotherapy
Cyclosporine/Tacrolimus↓Levels (induces CYP3A4)Monitor levels
Orlistat↓AbsorptionSeparate dosing by ≥2h
Colestipol/Cholestyramine↓AbsorptionSeparate dosing

7. Section 7: Special Populations

PopulationRecommendation
Preterm infants10–20 IU/kg/day IV/PO (TPN/formula); prevents haemolytic anaemia
Cystic fibrosis100–400 IU/day (water-miscible); monitor levels annually
Cholestasis/Biliary atresia15–25 IU/kg/day (water-miscible); monitor levels
AbetalipoproteinaemiaVit E 100–200 IU/kg/day (high-dose RRR); MCT oil, fat restriction
AVED (TTPA)α-tocopherol 800–1200 IU/day RRR; lifelong; monitors neurologic status
Short bowel / TPN10–20 IU/kg/day in TPN; monitor plasma α-tocopherol
Pregnancy/LactationRDA 15 mg/day; UL 1000 mg; no high-dose supplementation
ElderlyRDA 15 mg; dietary intake often low; consider supplement if fat malabsorption

ScenarioActionNotes

12y girl, progressive ataxia, areflexia, night blindness, lipids normal, vit E 0.5 mg/LGenetic test TTPA → AVED; high-dose RRR-α-toc 1200 IU/day lifelongAVED: TTPA mutation; early treatment halts progression
6m preterm (28w), haemolytic anaemia, Hb 8 g/dL, reticulocytosis, vit E lowVit E 15 IU/kg/day in TPN/formula; transfuse if Hb <7Prevents haemolytic anaemia; modern formulas supplemented
70M on warfarin, wants vit E supplement for "heart health"Advise against high-dose; if needed, ≤100 IU/day; monitor INR weeklyHigh-dose vit E ↑INR + antiplatelet → bleeding risk
55M post-MI, on simvastatin + niacin, considers vit E 400 IUAdvise against; vit E + vit C + β-carotene blunts HDL₂ rise with niacin+statinARBITER/HATS data; avoid antioxidant cocktails with lipid therapy
45M, considering vit E 400 IU for prostate cancer preventionAdvise AGAINST; SELECT trial: vit E 400 IU ↑prostate cancer HR 1.17Meta-analysis: no cancer prevention benefit; possible harm

5. 5. High-Yield FCPS/MRCP Points

[!important]
- Must know: 8 isoforms (α/β/γ/δ tocopherols + tocotrienols); α-TTP preferentially retains α-tocopherol; antioxidant mechanism (LOO• + α-TOH → LOOH + α-TO•; recycled by vit C/GSH); deficiency ONLY in fat malabsorption (CF, cholestasis, abetalipoproteinaemia, AVED); neurological triad (ataxia, neuropathy, retinopathy); AVED = TTPA mutation → high-dose oral vit E treats; high-dose (>400 IU) ↑mortality, ↑prostate cancer (SELECT), ↑haemorrhagic stroke
- Common viva: Difference abetalipoproteinaemia vs AVED (lipids vs normal lipids); α-TTP mechanism; why preterm haemolytic anaemia; mechanism of antioxidant recycling (vit C/GSH); SELECT trial outcome; warfarin interaction
- Exam trap: Recommending vit E for CVD prevention (no benefit, possible harm); confusing γ-tocopherol (dietary major) with α-tocopherol (biologically active); missing AVED diagnosis (normal lipids); using synthetic all-rac dose equivalence incorrectly

6. 6. Common Confusions / Exam Traps

TrapCorrection
Vit E prevents heart diseaseNO benefit (HOPE-TOO, GISSI, PHS II); possible ↑HF, ↑mortality
All isoforms equalα-TTP selects α-tocopherol; γ-tocopherol major in diet but rapidly metabolised
AVED = abetalipoproteinaemiaAVED: normal lipids/lipoproteins, TTPA mutation; Abetalipo: MTTP mutation, absent apoB, ↓cholesterol
Synthetic = natural doseAll-rac 2x dose needed for same bioactivity (1 mg all-rac = 1/2 RRR activity)
Preterm vit E = routineOnly <32w or <1500g need supplementation; modern formulas supplemented
Vit E + warfarin safe↑INR/bleeding risk; monitor if high-dose; antiplatelet effect additive
High-dose = better antioxidantPro-oxidant at high dose; tocopheroxyl radical not recycled; displaces γ-tocopherol

7. 7. Mnemonics

  • 8 Isoforms: 2×4 = 2 classes (tocopherols/tocotrienols) × 4 methylation states (α/β/γ/δ)
  • α-TTP selects: ALPHA TTP PICKS ALPHA → α-tocopherol preferential secretion
  • Antioxidant cycle: LOO• + E → LOOH + E• → VIT C/GSH RECYCLES E• (chain-breaking)
  • Deficiency causes: FAT MAL = Fat malabsorption (CF, cholestasis), Abetalipoproteinaemia (MTTP), TTPA mutation (AVED), M (MCT? no — malabsorption syndromes)
  • Neuro triad: ANR = Ataxia (spinocerebellar), Neuropathy (peripheral), Retinopathy (pigmented)
  • Abetalipo vs AVED: ABETALIPO = NO APOB, LOW CHOL; AVED = NORMAL LIPOPROTEINS, TTPA
  • SELECT trial: VIT E + SE → ↑PROSTATE CANCER (35k men, stopped early)
  • Mortality meta-analysis: >400 IU/day → ↑ALL-CAUSE MORTALITY (Miller 2005, Cochrane 2018)
  • Warfarin interaction: VIT E + WARFARIN = ↑INR (monitor)
  • Statins + Niacin + Antioxidants: VIT E/C/β-CAROTENE BLUNT HDL₂ RISE (ARBITER, HATS)

14. 14. Exam Answer Modes

1. Long Answer / Essay (20 min)

Topic: "Vitamin E: isoforms, antioxidant function, deficiency syndromes, and risks of supplementation"

  • 8 isoforms: 4 tocopherols (saturated) + 4 tocotrienols (unsaturated); α-tocopherol preferential retention by α-TTP
  • Antioxidant mechanism: chain-breaking lipid peroxidation (LOO• + α-TOH → LOOH + α-TO•); recycling by vitamin C/GSH
  • Deficiency ONLY in fat malabsorption: CF, cholestasis, abetalipoproteinaemia (MTTP → no apoB), AVED (TTPA → ↓α-TTP), preterm
  • Neurological triad: spinocerebellar ataxia, peripheral neuropathy, pigmented retinopathy
  • Abetalipoproteinaemia vs AVED: lipids absent vs normal; genes MTTP vs TTPA
  • High-dose risks: meta-analyses ↑mortality >400 IU/day (Miller 2005, Cochrane 2018); SELECT ↑prostate cancer; ↑haemorrhagic stroke; mechanism: pro-oxidant, displaces γ-tocopherol, antiplatelet
  • Drug interactions: warfarin (↑INR), statins+niacin (blunts HDL₂), antiplatelets (bleeding), chemo (theoretical)

2. Short Note (7 min)

Topic: "Abetalipoproteinaemia vs Isolated Vitamin E Deficiency (AVED)"

  • Abetalipoproteinaemia: MTTP mutation (AR) → no MTP → no chylomicron/VLDL assembly → absent apoB, LDL, VLDL; cholesterol <50 mg/dL; fat malabsorption + vit A/D/E/K deficiency; acanthocytosis, retinitis pigmentosa, ataxia, coagulopathy. Treatment: high-dose fat-soluble vitamins (vit E 100–200 IU/kg/day), MCT oil, fat restriction.
  • AVED: TTPA mutation (AR, chr 8) → ↓α-TTP → α-tocopherol not secreted in VLDL; normal lipids/lipoproteins; plasma α-toc <5% normal; onset 5–15y ataxia, neuropathy, retinopathy. Treatment: RRR-α-tocopherol 800–1200 IU/day lifelong → halts progression.

3. Viva Answer (3 min)

Q: "Why is vitamin E supplementation not recommended for cardiovascular prevention?"

"A: No benefit, possible harm. Large RCTs (HOPE-TOO, GISSI-P, PHS II) and meta-analyses (Cochrane 2018, 78 RCTs, 299k pts) show no reduction in CVD events; Cochrane: vit E ↑all-cause mortality RR 1.03. Proposed mechanisms: high-dose α-tocopherol displaces γ-tocopherol (loss of RNS trapping), pro-oxidant tocopheroxyl radical, antiplatelet effect. Do not prescribe for CVD prevention."

4. Ward Case Discussion (5 min)

Case: 8-year-old boy, progressive gait ataxia, areflexia, Babinski+, night blindness, normal lipid profile, plasma α-tocopherol 0.8 µmol/L (ref 11–37).

"Diagnosis: AVED (TTPA mutation). Confirm with genetic testing. Treatment: RRR-α-tocopherol 1200 IU/day (800–1500 IU) oral lifelong. Expect neurological stabilisation/improvement in 3–6 months. Refer for genetic counselling (AR, siblings 25% risk). Monitor plasma α-tocopherol target >20 µmol/L."

5. Last-Night-Before-Exam Sheet (1 min)

  • Isoforms: 4 tocopherols + 4 tocotrienols (α/β/γ/δ each); α-tocopherol biologically active
  • α-TTP: Liver protein → binds α-tocopherol → incorporates into VLDL; TTPA mutation = AVED
  • Antioxidant: LOO• + α-TOH → LOOH + α-TO• → recycled by vit C/GSH
  • Deficiency: ONLY fat malabsorption (CF, cholestasis, abetalipo, AVED, preterm)
  • Triad: Ataxia (spinocerebellar), Neuropathy (axonal sensory), Retinopathy (pigmented)
  • Abetalipo (MTTP): No apoB, low cholesterol, acanthocytes, all fat-soluble vit deficiency
  • AVED (TTPA): Normal lipids, plasma vit E <5%, Tx: RRR-α-toc 800–1200 IU/day
  • High-dose risks: >400 IU/day → ↑mortality (Miller), ↑prostate cancer (SELECT), ↑haemorrhagic stroke
  • Drug interactions: Warfarin (↑INR), statins+niacin (↓HDL₂), antiplatelets (bleeding)
  • Preterm: 10–20 IU/kg/day prevents haemolytic anaemia

  1. Abetalipoproteinaemia vs AVED key difference:

16. 16. SBA Questions (5)

  1. A 6-year-old boy with cystic fibrosis presents with progressive gait ataxia, loss of vibration sense, and night blindness. Plasma α-tocopherol 1.5 µmol/L (ref 11–37). Best management?

A. Dietary counselling only

B. Water-miscible vitamin E 400 IU/day; titrate to plasma α-tocopherol >20 µmol/L

C. Intramuscular vitamin E 100 IU weekly

D. High-dose oral RRR-α-tocopherol 1200 IU/day

E. Refer for gene therapy

  1. A 14-year-old girl presents with progressive ataxia, areflexia, and retinopathy. Lipid profile normal. Plasma α-tocopherol 0.6 µmol/L. Genetic testing confirms TTPA mutation. Treatment?

A. Vitamin E 100 IU/day

B. RRR-α-tocopherol 800–1200 IU/day lifelong

C. Intramuscular vitamin E monthly

D. Dietary modification only

E. Coenzyme Q10 supplementation

  1. A 28-week preterm infant develops haemolytic anaemia (Hb 7 g/dL, reticulocytes 15%, bilirubin 8 mg/dL). Vitamin E level low. Prevention in NICU?

A. Iron supplementation

B. Vitamin E 10–20 IU/kg/day in TPN/formula

C. Exchange transfusion

D. Erythropoietin

E. N-acetylcysteine

  1. A 65-year-old man on warfarin for atrial fibrillation starts vitamin E 400 IU/day for "heart health." INR rises from 2.5 to 4.2. Mechanism?

A. Vitamin E inhibits vitamin K epoxide reductase

B. Vitamin E has antiplatelet effect + may potentiate warfarin

C. Vitamin E induces CYP2C9

D. Vitamin E displaces warfarin from albumin

E. Vitamin E increases warfarin absorption

  1. A 55-year-old man on simvastatin 40 mg + extended-release niacin 2 g for low HDL-C considers adding vitamin E 400 IU. Why should this be avoided?

A. Vitamin E causes statin myopathy

B. Vitamin E + vitamin C + β-carotene blunt the HDL₂ rise from niacin-statin combination

C. Vitamin E increases statin hepatotoxicity

D. Vitamin E causes niacin flushing

E. No interaction; safe to add

17. 17. Flashcards

  • Q: 8 vitamin E isoforms

A: 4 tocopherols (α,β,γ,δ) + 4 tocotrienols (α,β,γ,δ)

  • Q: α-TTP function

A: Hepatic protein; binds α-tocopherol preferentially → incorporates into VLDL for plasma distribution

  • Q: Antioxidant mechanism

A: LOO• + α-TOH → LOOH + α-TO• (tocopheroxyl radical); α-TO• + Vit C/GSH → α-TOH (recycled)

  • Q: Deficiency ONLY in

A: Fat malabsorption syndromes (CF, cholestasis, abetalipo, AVED, preterm)

  • Q: Neuro triad

A: Spinocerebellar ataxia, Peripheral neuropathy (axonal sensory), Pigmented retinopathy

  • Q: Abetalipoproteinaemia

A: MTTP mutation → no MTP → no chylomicron/VLDL → absent apoB, LDL, VLDL; acanthocytes, retinitis, coagulopathy

  • Q: AVED (TTPA)

A: Normal lipids/lipoproteins; plasma α-toc <5% normal; Tx: RRR-α-toc 800–1200 IU/day lifelong

  • Q: Preterm haemolytic anaemia

A: Low vit E stores + high O₂ + iron → lipid peroxidation of RBC membranes; Tx: 10–20 IU/kg/day

  • Q: SELECT trial

A: 35,533 men; vit E 400 IU/day + Se 200 µg → ↑prostate cancer HR 1.17; stopped early

  • Q: Meta-analysis >400 IU/day

A: ↑All-cause mortality (RR 1.03–1.04); no CVD benefit (Cochrane 2018, 78 RCTs, 299k)

  • Q: Warfarin interaction

A: ↑INR/bleeding risk; antiplatelet effect (↓TXA₂, ↑PGI₂)

  • Q: Statins + Niacin + Antioxidants

A: ARBITER/HATS: vit E/C/β-carotene blunt HDL₂ rise from niacin+statin

  • Q: UL & bioactivity

A: UL 1000 mg (1500 IU natural); RRR-α-toc 2x bioactivity of all-rac

  • Q: γ-tocopherol

A: Major dietary form (soybean/corn oil); traps RNS (NO₂•, ONOO⁻); anti-inflammatory

  • Q: Synthetic vs natural

A: All-rac (dl-α-toc) = 8 stereoisomers; only 1/8 is RRR; 2x dose needed for same bioactivity

18. 18. Answer Key with Explanations

1. MCQs

  1. B — α-tocopherol has highest biological activity and is preferentially retained by α-TTP.
  2. B — α-TTP (TTPA gene product) binds α-tocopherol in hepatocyte → transfers to VLDL for secretion.
  3. B — Vitamin E is a chain-breaking antioxidant: donates H to lipid peroxyl radical (LOO•), forming stable tocopheroxyl radical; recycled by vit C/GSH.
  4. B — Deficiency essentially only occurs with fat malabsorption; dietary deficiency alone extremely rare.
  5. B — Classic triad: spinocerebellar ataxia, peripheral neuropathy (axonal, sensory), pigmented retinopathy.
  6. B — AVED = TTPA mutation → α-TTP defect; MTTP = abetalipoproteinaemia.
  7. B — Abetalipoproteinaemia: MTTP mutation → no apoB → absent chylomicrons/VLDL → very low cholesterol/triglycerides; AVED: normal lipids.
  8. B — SELECT: vit E 400 IU/day + Se 200 µg → ↑prostate cancer HR 1.17 (99% CI 1.004–1.36); stopped early.
  9. B — Miller 2005 (19 RCTs, 136k) & Cochrane 2018 (78 RCTs, 299k): vit E ≥400 IU/day → ↑all-cause mortality RR 1.03–1.04.
  10. B — Vit E has antiplatelet effect (↓TXA₂, ↑PGI₂) → potentiates warfarin → ↑INR, bleeding risk.

2. SBAs

  1. B — CF-related vit E deficiency: water-miscible preparation 400 IU/day, monitor plasma α-tocopherol target >20 µmol/L; pancreatic enzymes + fat-soluble vitamin supplementation.
  2. B — AVED (TTPA confirmed): high-dose RRR-α-tocopherol 800–1200 IU/day lifelong; normal lipids differentiate from abetalipo.
  3. B — Preterm <32w/≤1500g: vit E 10–20 IU/kg/day in TPN/formula prevents haemolytic anaemia.
  4. B — Antiplatelet effect (↓TXA₂, ↑PGI₂) → ↓platelet aggregation → potentiates warfarin anticoagulation → ↑INR/bleeding.
  5. B — ARBITER/HATS: antioxidant vitamins (E, C, β-carotene) blunt niacin-induced HDL₂ rise when added to statin therapy.

19. 19. Summary

Vitamin E is a Must Know 🔴 topic for FCPS/MRCP.

Key takeaway: 8 isoforms; α-tocopherol biologically active (α-TTP retention); chain-breaking antioxidant (LOO• + α-TOH → recycled by vit C/GSH); deficiency ONLY in fat malabsorption (CF, cholestasis, abetalipoproteinaemia, AVED, preterm); neurological triad (ataxia, neuropathy, retinopathy); abetalipoproteinaemia (MTTP, no apoB) vs AVED (TTPA, normal lipids); AVED treated with high-dose RRR-α-tocopherol 800–1200 IU/day. High-dose (>400 IU) HARMFUL: Meta-analyses ↑all-cause mortality, SELECT ↑prostate cancer, ↑haemorrhagic stroke. Drug interactions: warfarin (↑INR), statins+niacin (blunts HDL₂), antiplatelets.

Exam focus: Isoforms, α-TTP, antioxidant mechanism, deficiency triad, abetalipo vs AVED, high-dose risks, SELECT trial, drug interactions.

Clinical relevance: CF/cholestasis vit E supplementation; AVED diagnosis/treatment; avoiding unnecessary vit E supplementation; warfarin monitoring.

topics.title: Vitamin K Coagulation Bone Metabolism
topics.html:
Related: [[Nutritional Factors in Disease MOC]], [[Davidson Chapter 22 - Nutritional Factors in Disease Hierarchy]], [[../00_Index/Medicine MOC|Medicine MOC]]

[!important]
Vitamin K = K1 (phylloquinone, plants) + K2 (menaquinones MK-4 to MK-13, bacterial/animal); γ-glutamyl carboxylase cofactor → Gla residues on clotting factors II, VII, IX, X + proteins C, S; warfarin antagonises VKORC1; deficiency = bleeding (↑PT/INR); bone: osteocalcin/MGP carboxylation.

1. 1. Learning Objectives

  • [ ] Distinguish vitamin K1 (phylloquinone), K2 (menaquinones MK-n), K3 (menadione) sources and bioavailability
  • [ ] Explain γ-glutamyl carboxylase mechanism: Glu → Gla (Ca²⁺-binding); VKORC1 recycles vitamin K epoxide → hydroquinone
  • [ ] Describe warfarin mechanism: inhibits VKORC1 → ↓γ-carboxylation → dysfunctional clotting factors (↑PT/INR first, factor VII t½ 6h)
  • [ ] Identify vitamin K deficiency causes: neonates (HDN), malabsorption, broad-spectrum antibiotics, warfarin
  • [ ] State HDN prophylaxis: 1 mg phytomenadione IM at birth; treatment: 1 mg IV/PO
  • [ ] Outline bone role: osteocalcin (bone formation marker), matrix Gla protein (MGP, inhibits vascular calcification)
  • [ ] Compare MK-7 (natto) vs K1: longer half-life, better extrahepatic distribution, bone benefit
  • [ ] Recognise coumarin resistance: VKORC1 mutation; superwarfarins (brodifacoum) long half-life

2. 2. Definitions / Key Concepts

TermDefinition
Phylloquinone (K1)Plant form (green leafy veg, plant oils); major dietary source (~90% intake); side chain: phytyl (saturated)
Menaquinones (K2, MK-n)Bacterial/animal forms; side chain: unsaturated isoprenoid (n repeats); MK-4 (animal tissues), MK-7 (natto, fermented foods)
Menadione (K3)Synthetic naphthoquinone; no side chain; water-soluble; toxic (haemolysis, kernicterus) — not used therapeutically

Gla Residueγ-carboxyglutamic acid; binds Ca²⁺ via dicarboxylic acid; essential for protein function (clotting, bone, vascular)
VKORC1Vitamin K epoxide reductase complex subunit 1; recycles vitamin K epoxide → quinone → hydroquinone (active cofactor); warfarin target
Vitamin K CycleKH₂ (hydroquinone) → GGCX (Glu→Gla) → KO (epoxide) → VKORC1 (KO→KH₂) → repeat
PhytomenadioneSynthetic K1 analogue; used for prophylaxis/treatment (IM, IV, PO); Konakion MM (mixed micellar) for IV

Osteocalcin (Bone Gla Protein, BGP)Osteoblast product; γ-carboxylated (3 Gla); binds hydroxyapatite; serum = bone formation marker; uncarboxylated (ucOC) = vit K status
Matrix Gla Protein (MGP)Vascular smooth muscle/chondrocyte product; γ-carboxylated (5 Gla); inhibits vascular calcification (binds Ca crystals); MGP-knockout → arterial calcification
Protein C & SVitamin K-dependent anticoagulants; Protein C (activated by thrombomodulin-thrombin) → inactivates Va/VIIa; Protein S = cofactor

aPTTActivated partial thromboplastin time; intrinsic pathway; normal in early vit K deficiency, prolonged if severe

3. 3. Core Content

1. Section 1: Forms, Sources & Absorption

Vitamin K1 (Phylloquinone): Green leafy vegetables (spinach, kale, broccoli, Brussels sprouts), plant oils (soybean, canola). Contributes ~90% dietary intake. Bioavailability: 5–10% from vegetables (matrix-bound), higher with fat/mechanical disruption. Absorption: mixed micelles → enterocyte → chylomicron.

Vitamin K2 (Menaquinones MK-n): Bacterial synthesis (gut microbiota: MK-6 to MK-13), animal tissues (MK-4 from menadione in feed), fermented foods (natto = MK-7, cheese = MK-8/MK-9). MK-4 in meat, dairy, eggs. MK-7 (natto) — long half-life (72h vs K1 1–2h), better extrahepatic distribution.

Menadione (K3): Synthetic, water-soluble; converts to MK-4 in tissues; toxic (haemolysis, kernicterus in neonates) — NOT used clinically.

Absorption: Requires bile salts, pancreatic lipase; chylomicron secretion; lymphatic transport. K1: hepatic uptake via chylomicron remnant; MK-7: longer circulation, extrahepatic delivery.

2. Section 2: γ-Glutamyl Carboxylase & Vitamin K Cycle

Reaction: Glu (target protein) + CO₂ + O₂ + KH₂ (vitamin K hydroquinone) → Gla + KO (vitamin K epoxide) + H₂O.

Enzyme: γ-glutamyl carboxylase (GGCX); ER membrane-bound; processive (multiple Glu per substrate).

Vitamin K Cycle:

  1. KH₂ (hydroquinone) = active cofactor for GGCX
  2. GGCX uses KH₂ → carboxylates Glu → Gla → produces KO (epoxide)
  3. VKORC1 reduces KO → K (quinone) → KH₂ (hydroquinone) using NAD(P)H
  4. Warfarin inhibits VKORC1 → KO accumulates → KH₂ depleted → ↓γ-carboxylation

Target proteins (17 human Gla-proteins):

  • Coagulation: Factors II (prothrombin, 10 Gla), VII (10 Gla), IX (12 Gla), X (11 Gla)
  • Anticoagulation: Protein C (9 Gla), Protein S (11 Gla), Protein Z
  • Bone: Osteocalcin (BGP, 3 Gla), Matrix Gla Protein (MGP, 5 Gla)
  • Other: Gas6, PRGP1/2, TMG3/4, periostin

3. Section 3: Warfarin Pharmacology

Mechanism: Competitive inhibitor of VKORC1 (binds epoxide binding site); prevents KO → KH₂ recycling.

Onset: Delayed (factor half-lives): Factor VII t½ 6h (PT ↑ first, 12–24h), IX t½ 24h, X t½ 40h, II t½ 60h; full effect 3–5 days.

Monitoring: INR (target therapeutic range: 2.0–3.0 most indications, 2.5–3.5 mechanical mitral valve).

Dosing: Genetic (CYP2C9 2/3 poor metaboliser, VKORC1 -1639G>A low expression), age, weight, diet, interacting drugs.

Reversal: Vitamin K1 (phytomenadione) 1–10 mg IV/PO (IV: faster, anaphylaxis risk <0.1%); PCC (4-factor) for urgent; FFP if PCC unavailable.

Interactions:

  • ↑INR: Antibiotics (↓gut flora K2), amiodarone, metronidazole, TMP-SMX azoles, PPIs, cranberry, NSAIDs
  • ↓INR: Enzyme inducers (rifampicin, carbamazepine, phenytoin, barbiturates, St John's wort), vitamin K-rich foods

4. Section 4: Vitamin K Deficiency

Adults (rare):

  • Malabsorption: Coeliac, Crohn's, CF, biliary obstruction, short bowel, bariatric
  • Antibiotics: Broad-spectrum (3rd gen cephalosporins, carbapenems) → ↓gut flora MK synthesis
  • Warfarin: Intentional iatrogenic deficiency
  • TPN without vitamin K: Weeks without supplementation
  • Severe malnutrition

Neonates (HDN):

  • Physiological: Low placental transfer, sterile gut, low breast milk K1 (1–4 µg/L vs formula 50–100 µg/L)
  • Classical HDN: Day 1–7; bleeding (umbilical, GI, circumcision, intracranial)
  • Late HDN: 2–12 weeks; exclusively breastfed; cholestasis, malabsorption, antibiotics; intracranial haemorrhage 30–50%
  • Prevention: 1 mg phytomenadione IM at birth (universal); oral alternative: 2 mg at birth, 2 mg day 4–7, 2 mg week 4 (less reliable)

Clinical features: Bleeding (ecchymosis, haematuria, GI bleed, intracranial), ↑PT/INR (early), ↑aPTT (late), normal platelet count, normal fibrinogen, ↑PIVKA-II.

5. Section 5: Treatment of Deficiency

6. Section 6: Bone & Vascular Metabolism

Osteocalcin (BGP): Osteoblast product; 3 Gla residues; γ-carboxylation → binds hydroxyapatite (Ca²⁺); serum osteocalcin = bone formation marker; uncarboxylated osteocalcin (ucOC) ↑ with vit K deficiency; total osteocalcin reflects turnover.

Matrix Gla Protein (MGP): Vascular smooth muscle cells, chondrocytes; 5 Gla; potent inhibitor of vascular calcification (binds Ca-phosphate crystals, inhibits BMP-2); MGP-knockout mice → severe arterial calcification, death by 6w.

Vitamin K & Bone Health: Observational: low K intake → ↑fracture risk, ↓BMD; RCTs: K1 1–5 mg/day or MK-4 45 mg/day (Japan) → ↓fracture (hip/vertebral); MK-7 (natto) 180 µg/day → ↓ucOC, ↑BMD.

Vascular Calcification: MGP requires γ-carboxylation to inhibit calcification; warfarin → ↓MGP carboxylation → ↑vascular calcification (animal models; human observational).

MK-7 vs K1: MK-7 (natto) longer half-life, better extrahepatic distribution (bone, vessel), more potent at carboxylating osteocalcin/MGP at lower doses.

5. 5. High-Yield FCPS/MRCP Points

[!important]
- Must know: K1 (phylloquinone) vs K2 (menaquinones MK-n); γ-glutamyl carboxylase → Gla residues; VKORC1 recycles epoxide → hydroquinone; warfarin inhibits VKORC1; HDN prophylaxis 1 mg IM at birth; late HDN in breastfed/cholestasis → ICH; PT/INR first abnormal (factor VII t½ 6h); PIVKA-II sensitive marker; osteocalcin/MGP carboxylation for bone/vascular health; MK-7 longer half-life, better extrahepatic
- Common viva: Warfarin mechanism (VKORC1); why PT ↑ before aPTT; HDN types (classical vs late); reversal of warfarin overdose (PCC + vit K); coumarin resistance VKORC1; osteocalcin vs ucOC; superwarfarin management
- Exam trap: Giving IM vit K in anticoagulated patient (haematoma risk — use IV/PO); confusing factor half-lives (VII 6h, II 60h); thinking aPTT first in vit K deficiency; missing late HDN in cholestatic infant; warfarin + antibiotics = ↑INR (give K1 prophylactically? no, just monitor)

7. 7. Mnemonics

  • K forms: K1 = PLANTS (phylloquinone), K2 = BACTERIA/ANIMALS (menaquinones MK-n), K3 = SYNTHETIC TOXIC (menadione)
  • γ-Carboxylase: GGCX = Glu → Gla (needs CO₂, O₂, KH₂); produces KO (epoxide)
  • VKORC1: RECYCLES KO → KH₂; WARFARIN INHIBITS VKORC1
  • Coagulation factors: 1972 = 10 Gla (X), 9 Gla (VII), 7 (IX), 2 (II) — wait: II=10, VII=10, IX=12, X=11 → Ten-Ten-Twelve-Eleven
  • Anticoagulant proteins: C & S = Coagulation inhibition (Protein C & S + Protein Z)
  • Factor half-lives: VII SIX TEN TWO = 7, 6, 24, 40, 60 (VII 6h, IX 24h, X 40h, II 60h) — shortest VII
  • HDN: CLASSICAL = Day 1–7; LATE = 2–12 weeks (breastfed + cholestasis) → ICH
  • PT first: PT = Prothrombin (factor VII) Test = extrinsic; Shortest t½
  • Warfarin reversal: PCC = Immediate (factors); K1 = Sustained (days); Bleeding = BOTH
  • Bone: Osteocalcin = Bone formation marker; ucOC = uncarboxylated = vit K status
  • MGP: Matrix Gla Protein = Major Guard against Pathologic calcification
  • Superwarfarin: BRODIFACOUM = Big dose K1, Really long (weeks-months), Oral/IV, Daily, INR monitor, For Antidote, Continue, On and Until normal, Months
