9. 9. -Hour Recall Prompts

  1. 1-carbon transfer: dUMP→dTMP (5,10-methylene-THF); homocysteine→methionine (5-methyl-THF + B12)
  2. Deficiency causes: poor diet, MTX, trimethoprim, OCP, alcohol, haemolysis, pregnancy
  3. Megaloblastic anaemia: macrocytic MCV >100, hypersegmented neutrophils (earliest), pancytopenia
  4. NTD prevention: 400 µg/day standard, 5 mg if high risk; preconception 1 month
  5. Folate vs B12: MMA normal in folate (↑ in B12); neuro only in B12
  6. Methotrexate rescue: leucovorin (5-formyl-THF) 15 mg IV/PO 24h after MTX
  7. MTHFR C677T: most common folate polymorphism; ↑homocysteine
  8. OCP/HRT depletes folate; periconception 400 µg

10. 10. -Day / 15-Day / 30-Day Revision Tracker

DayDateRecall Quality (1-5)Time SpentNotes
1
7
15
30

11. 11. Must Know / Should Know / Nice to Know

PriorityContent
Must Know 🔴1-carbon transfer (dTMP, methionine); megaloblastic anaemia (macrocytic, hypersegmented neutrophils); deficiency causes (MTX, TMP, OCP, alcohol, pregnancy); NTD prevention (400 µg / 5 mg); check B12 before folate; normal MMA in folate; methotrexate rescue (leucovorin)
Should Know 🟡MTHFR C677T; MTRR mutation; OCP folate depletion; fortification programmes; valproate NTD risk; MTHFD1 polymorphisms; absorption (PCFT pH)
Nice to Know 🟢5-Formyl-THF (leucovorin) rescue protocols; polyglutamylation; methyl-trap hypothesis; folate in neurodevelopmental disorders; Hcy-cysteine remethylation genetics

12. 12. My Weak Points

  • [ ] MTHFR C677T prevalence by ethnicity
  • [ ] Methotrexate rescue dose timing detail
  • [ ] PCFT/SLC46A1 hereditary malabsorption

13. 13. Self-Test Scorecard

DomainScore /10Target /10
Understanding8+
Recall8+
MCQ Performance8+
SBA Performance8+
Viva Confidence8+
TOTAL40+/50

14. 14. Exam Answer Modes

1. Long Answer / Essay (20 min)

Topic: "Folate: biochemistry, deficiency, and clinical significance"

  • Biochemistry: 1-carbon transfer (THF cofactor); dUMP→dTMP (TS, 5,10-methylene-THF); homocysteine→methionine (MS, 5-methyl-THF, B12); purine synthesis
  • Deficiency: megaloblastic anaemia (MCV >100, hypersegmented neutrophils, pancytopenia); GI (glossitis); NTD in pregnancy
  • Causes: poor diet, alcohol, malabsorption, drugs (MTX, TMP, OCP, anticonvulsants), ↑demand
  • NTD prevention: 400 µg/day periconception; 5 mg if high risk (previous NTD, valproate, DM)
  • Diagnosis: ↓serum/red cell folate, ↑homocysteine, normal MMA; check B12 first (folate hides B12)
  • Treatment: 5 mg/day ×4 months; leucovorin rescue for MTX toxicity

2. Short Note (7 min)

Topic: "Folate vs B12 Deficiency"

  • Both: Macrocytic megaloblastic anaemia, hypersegmented neutrophils, pancytopenia, ↑homocysteine
  • B12 only: ↑MMA (sensitive/specific); subacute combined degeneration (posterior + lateral columns, peripheral neuropathy, dementia)
  • Folate only: Risk of NTD; very rare neurological
  • Critical: Folate treatment in B12 deficiency worsens neurological disease (irreversible) despite improving anaemia
  • Practical: Always check B12 (or co-prescribe B12) when starting folate for macrocytic anaemia

3. Viva Answer (3 min)

Q: "Why is folate given before conception?"

"A: Neural tube closure occurs days 21–28 post-conception — often before pregnancy confirmed. NTD (anencephaly, spina bifida) result from failed closure. Folate required for DNA synthesis/cell proliferation during neurulation. 400 µg/day from 1 month preconception through 12 weeks gestation; 5 mg if high risk (previous NTD, valproate, DM, obesity). Fortification programmes ↓NTD 20–50%."

4. Ward Case Discussion (5 min)

Case: 30F on MTX 15 mg weekly for RA, 6 months, presents with macrocytic anaemia (Hb 9, MCV 110), hypersegmented neutrophils, mouth ulcers. B12 normal.

"Diagnosis: MTX-induced functional folate deficiency (DHFR inhibition). Treatment: Folic acid 5 mg/week 24 hours after MTX (separate dose to avoid competitive inhibition). Monitor Hb, MCV. Mouth ulcers improve with folate. Alternative: leucovorin 15 mg rescue for severe toxicity."

5. Last-Night-Before-Exam Sheet (1 min)

  • 1-Carbon transfer: dUMP→dTMP (TS, 5,10-methylene-THF); Hcy→Met (MS, 5-methyl-THF, B12); purine (C2, C8)
  • Megaloblastic: Macrocytic, hypersegmented neutrophils (earliest), pancytopenia, ↑LDH
  • Causes: Diet, alcohol, malabsorption, MTX, TMP, OCP, anticonvulsants, ↑demand
  • NTD prevention: 400 µg standard / 5 mg high risk; preconception 1 month
  • B12 vs Folate: MMA ↑ in B12 only; neuro only in B12; CHECK B12 BEFORE FOLATE
  • MTX rescue: Leucovorin (5-formyl-THF) 15 mg 24h after MTX
  • OCP: ↓folate absorption + ↑plasma clearance; consider 400 µg supplement
  • Fortification: 80+ countries; ↓NTD 20–50%

15. 15. MCQs (10)

  1. Major circulating form of folate:

A. Folic acid

B. DHF

C. THF

D. 5-Methyl-THF

  1. Folate cofactor for thymidylate synthase (dUMP → dTMP):

A. 5-Methyl-THF

B. 5,10-Methylene-THF

C. N¹⁰-formyl-THF

D. THF

  1. Neural tube closure occurs:

A. Days 7–14 post-conception

B. Days 21–28 post-conception

C. Weeks 8–12 gestation

D. Trimester 2

  1. Standard folic acid dose for NTD prevention (no risk factors):

A. 100 µg/day

B. 400 µg/day

C. 1 mg/day

D. 5 mg/day

  1. Folic acid dose in women with previous NTD or on valproate:

A. 400 µg/day

B. 1 mg/day

C. 5 mg/day

D. 10 mg/day

  1. Biochemical marker elevated in BOTH folate and B12 deficiency:

A. Methylmalonic acid (MMA)

B. Homocysteine

C. Methylmalonic acid AND homocysteine

D. Neither

  1. Biochemical marker SPECIFIC for B12 deficiency:

A. Homocysteine

B. Methylmalonic acid (MMA)

C. Cystathionine

D. Methionine

  1. Methotrexate rescue agent (bypass DHFR):

A. Folic acid

B. Folinic acid (leucovorin, 5-formyl-THF)

C. 5-methyl-THF

D. Pyridoxine

  1. Drug causing folate deficiency by ↓absorption + ↑clearance:

A. Methotrexate

B. Trimethoprim

C. Oral contraceptive pill

D. Phenytoin

  1. Earliest sign of megaloblastic anaemia:

A. Macrocytosis

B. Hypersegmented neutrophils (≥5 lobes)

C. Pancytopenia

D. ↑MCV

16. 16. SBA Questions (5)

  1. A 32-year-old woman with previous anencephalic pregnancy is planning another pregnancy. What folate dose should she take periconceptionally?

A. 400 µg/day

B. 1 mg/day

C. 5 mg/day

D. 10 mg/day

E. No folate needed

  1. A 60-year-old man on methotrexate 15 mg weekly for rheumatoid arthritis develops macrocytic anaemia (Hb 9.5, MCV 105), hypersegmented neutrophils, and mouth ulcers. B12 normal. Management?

A. Stop methotrexate permanently

B. Folic acid 5 mg/week, taken 24 hours after methotrexate

C. Leucovorin 200 mg IV

D. Folic acid 5 mg daily

E. Switch to alternative DMARD

  1. A 28-year-old woman on valproate for epilepsy is planning pregnancy. Folate advice?

A. No folate needed

B. 400 µg/day throughout pregnancy

C. 5 mg/day preconception and 1st trimester; consider AED switch

D. Folic acid 10 mg daily

E. Folic acid IV

  1. A 50-year-old man with macrocytic anaemia (Hb 8.5, MCV 115), hypersegmented neutrophils. B12 is 90 pmol/L (low). Methylmalonic acid 2.5 µmol/L (very high), homocysteine 60 µmol/L. Diagnosis?

A. Folate deficiency

B. B12 deficiency

C. Combined folate + B12 deficiency

D. Anaemia of chronic disease

E. Myelodysplasia

  1. A 35-year-old woman of childbearing age with no pregnancy plan and on OCP. What dietary supplement should be considered?

A. No supplement

B. Folic acid 400 µg/day

C. Iron

D. Vitamin D

E. Vitamin C

17. 17. Flashcards

  • Q: 1-Carbon transfer: dTMP

A: 5,10-methylene-THF (thymidylate synthase, dUMP→dTMP)

  • Q: 1-Carbon transfer: Methionine

A: 5-methyl-THF (methionine synthase, Hcy→Met, B12 cofactor)

  • Q: Megaloblastic earliest sign

A: Hypersegmented neutrophils (≥5 lobes) before anaemia

  • Q: NTD prevention standard

A: 400 µg/day, 1 month preconception → 12 weeks gestation

  • Q: NTD prevention high risk

A: 5 mg/day (previous NTD, valproate, DM, obesity, haemolysis)

  • Q: Folate vs B12 MMA

A: MMA NORMAL in folate; ↑↑ in B12 (sensitive/specific)

  • Q: Folate vs B12 neuro

A: Neuro only in B12 (SCD); folate hides anaemia, worsens B12 neuro

  • Q: MTX rescue

A: Leucovorin 15 mg 24h after MTX (5-formyl-THF bypasses DHFR)

  • Q: Drugs depleting folate

A: MS-TOP-OCP = MTX, Sulfasalazine, Trimethoprim, OCP, Pyrimethamine + Anticonvulsants

  • Q: MTHFR C677T

A: Most common folate polymorphism; ↑homocysteine; risk CVD/stroke

  • Q: Folate excess effect

A: No toxicity (water-soluble); but may hide B12 deficiency (anaemia correct, neuro progresses)

18. 18. Answer Key with Explanations

1. MCQs

  1. D — 5-methyl-THF is the major circulating form; THF accepts one-carbon units at N⁵ or N¹⁰ positions.
  2. B — 5,10-methylene-THF provides carbon for dUMP → dTMP via thymidylate synthase (rate-limiting DNA synthesis step).
  3. B — Neural tube closure days 21–28 post-conception (3–4 weeks gestation); before pregnancy often confirmed; hence periconception folate.
  4. B — Standard 400 µg/day from 1 month preconception through 12 weeks gestation; 5 mg if risk factors.
  5. C — High risk (previous NTD, valproate/carbamazepine, DM, obesity): 5 mg/day.
  6. B — Homocysteine ↑ in BOTH folate and B12 deficiency; MMA ↑ ONLY in B12 (key differentiator).
  7. B — Methylmalonic acid (MMA) is sensitive and specific for B12 deficiency; accumulated due to impaired methylmalonyl-CoA mutase.
  8. B — Folinic acid (5-formyl-THF, leucovorin) is reduced form; bypasses DHFR block from methotrexate.
  9. C — OCP decreases folate absorption and increases plasma clearance; contributes to megaloblastic changes.
  10. B — Hypersegmented neutrophils (≥5 lobes) appear in blood before macrocytosis/anaemia; earliest sign.

2. SBAs

  1. C — Previous NTD = 5 mg/day preconception + 1st trimester; 10x recurrence risk.
  2. B — MTX-induced folate deficiency: folic acid 5 mg/week 24h after MTX (separate dose to avoid competitive DHFR inhibition); mouth ulcers improve.
  3. C — Valproate (highest NTD risk of AEDs) + periconception folate 5 mg/day; consider switch to lamotrigine/levetiracetam if planning pregnancy.
  4. B — Low B12 + ↑↑MMA (specific) + ↑Hcy = B12 deficiency; folate deficiency would have normal MMA.
  5. B — OCP depletes folate; 400 µg/day periconception recommended for all women of childbearing age (also covers unplanned pregnancy).

19. 19. Summary

Folate (B9) is a Must Know 🔴 topic for FCPS/MRCP.

Key takeaway: 1-carbon transfer: dUMP→dTMP (DNA synthesis via TS) + homocysteine→methionine (methylation via MS, B12 cofactor). Deficiency = megaloblastic anaemia (macrocytic, hypersegmented neutrophils), glossitis, NTD in pregnancy. Causes: diet, alcohol, MTX, trimethoprim, OCP, anticonvulsants, ↑demand (pregnancy/haemolysis). NTD prevention: 400 µg/day standard, 5 mg high risk (previous NTD, valproate, DM), periconception 1 month. Check B12 before folate (folate hides B12 anaemia, worsens B12 neuro). Methotrexate rescue: leucovorin 15 mg 24h after.

Exam focus: 1-carbon reactions, megaloblastic vs B12 (MMA, neuro), NTD prevention, MTX rescue, MTHFR, OCP effect.

Clinical relevance: Universal periconception folate; MTX monitoring; B12 check before folate; OCP counselling.

Template version: 1.0 | Davidson 24e Ch 22 aligned | FCPS/MRCP oriented

topics.title: Pyridoxine B6 Biotin Pantothenic Acid
topics.html:
Related: [[Nutritional Factors in Disease MOC]], [[Davidson Chapter 22 - Nutritional Factors in Disease Hierarchy]], [[../00_Index/Medicine MOC|Medicine MOC]]

[!important]
Pyridoxine (B6) = PLP cofactor for 140+ enzymes (transamination, decarboxylation, glycogen phosphorylase, haem synthesis); deficiency = sideroblastic anaemia, peripheral neuropathy, seizures, cheilosis, glossitis; Biotin = carboxylase cofactor; deficiency = dermatitis, alopecia, conjunctivitis; Pantothenic acid = CoA/ACP; deficiency RARE (burning feet syndrome).

1. 1. Learning Objectives

  • [ ] Describe pyridoxine forms (PN, PM, PL) → phosphorylation → PLP (active); PLP-dependent enzyme classes
  • [ ] List pyridoxine deficiency causes: INH, penicillamine, hydralazine, oral contraceptives, alcohol, malabsorption, dialysis
  • [ ] Recognise pyridoxine deficiency: sideroblastic anaemia (↓ALAS2), peripheral neuropathy (↓GABA), seizures (infants), cheilosis, glossitis, hyperirritability
  • [ ] Explain pyridoxine toxicity: sensory neuronopathy (dorsal column) at >100 mg/day chronic; reversible on stopping
  • [ ] Describe biotin → carboxylases (ACC, PCC, MCC, PC); deficiency: raw egg whites (avidin), TPN, antibiotics, genetic (biotinidase/holocarboxylase synthetase)
  • [ ] State biotin deficiency: scaly dermatitis (periorificial), alopecia, conjunctivitis, metabolic acidosis, organic aciduria
  • [ ] Explain pantothenic acid → CoA (acyl carrier) + ACP (fatty acid synthase); deficiency EXTREMELY RARE (burning feet syndrome, fatigue)

2. 2. Definitions / Key Concepts

TermDefinition
Pyridoxine (PN)Alcohol form; dietary; converted to PLP
Pyridoxal (PL)Aldehyde form; phosphorylated to PLP
Pyridoxamine (PM)Amine form; transamination intermediate
PLP (Pyridoxal 5'-Phosphate)Active cofactor; Schiff base with lysine ε-amino group; >140 enzymes
TransaminationPLP-dependent; amino acid + α-ketoacid ↔ new amino acid + new α-ketoacid; ALT, AST
DecarboxylationPLP-dependent: DOPA → dopamine (aromatic L-amino acid decarboxylase), 5-HTP → serotonin, glutamate → GABA (GAD), histidine → histamine
Glycogen PhosphorylasePLP-dependent; glycogen → glucose-1-P; muscle/liver; PLP = structural (not catalytic)
ALAS2 (δ-ALA Synthase 2)PLP-dependent; mitochondrial; glycine + succinyl-CoA → δ-ALA (haem synthesis); deficiency → sideroblastic anaemia
Sideroblastic AnaemiaRing sideroblasts (Fe-loaded mitochondria around nucleus); microcytic/hypochromic/dimorphic; ↑ferritin, ↑Fe, ↓TIBC
PLP Deficiency → ↓GABAGlutamate decarboxylase (GAD) requires PLP; ↓GABA → seizures (esp. infants), hyperirritability
Pyridoxine ToxicitySensory neuronopathy (dorsal column: loss vibration/position sense, ataxia); dose >100 mg/day chronic; reversible on cessation
INH/Penicillamine/Hydralazine/OCPForm hydrazones with PLP → ↑renal excretion; INH also inhibits pyridoxal kinase
Biotin (B7)Carboxylase cofactor: ATP + HCO₃⁻ + substrate → carboxylated substrate; holocarboxylase synthetase attaches to lysine
Acetyl-CoA Carboxylase (ACC)Acetyl-CoA → malonyl-CoA (rate-limiting fatty acid synthesis)
Pyruvate Carboxylase (PC)Pyruvate → oxaloacetate (anaplerosis, gluconeogenesis)
Propionyl-CoA Carboxylase (PCC)Propionyl-CoA → methylmalonyl-CoA (odd-chain FA, BCAA, cholesterol)
Methylcrotonyl-CoA Carboxylase (MCC)Leucine catabolism
AvidinRaw egg white glycoprotein; binds biotin 10¹⁵ affinity → biotin deficiency; heat denatures avidin
Biotinidase DeficiencyBTD mutation (AR) → cannot recycle biotin; late-onset (weeks–months); seizures, hypotonia, dermatitis, alopecia, organic aciduria
Holocarboxylase Synthetase DeficiencyHLCS mutation (AR) → cannot attach biotin to carboxylases; early-onset (neonatal); severe
Pantothenic Acid (B5)Component of CoA (4'-phosphopantetheine) + ACP (fatty acid synthase); ubiquitous in foods

3. 3. Core Content

1. Section 1: Pyridoxine (B6) Metabolism

Forms: Pyridoxine (PN, alcohol), pyridoxal (PL, aldehyde), pyridoxamine (PM, amine); interconvertible.

Absorption: Jejunum; passive diffusion; phosphorylated by pyridoxal kinase → PLP (liver, RBC, brain).

PLP-dependent enzymes (6 classes):

  1. Transaminases: ALT, AST, BCAT (BCAA) — amino group transfer
  2. Decarboxylases: DOPA decarboxylase, 5-HTP decarboxylase, GAD (glutamate → GABA), histidine decarboxylase
  3. Glycogen phosphorylase: Structural PLP; glycogenolysis
  4. Haem synthesis: ALAS2 (δ-ALA synthase 2) — erythroid-specific
  5. Cystathionine β-synthase (CBS): Homocysteine + serine → cystathionine (transsulfuration); PLP + B12 (methylcobalamin) for homocysteine remethylation
  6. Kynureninase: Tryptophan → niacin pathway; INH/B6 deficiency → inhibition

Catabolism: PLP → pyridoxic acid (excreted in urine); 4-pyridoxic acid = major metabolite.

2. Section 2: Pyridoxine Deficiency

Causes: INH, penicillamine, hydralazine, isoniazid, OCP, alcohol, malabsorption, dialysis, pregnancy, lactation, 5-FU, cycloserine, theophylline.

Clinical Features:

SystemFeatures
HaematologicalSideroblastic anaemia (↓ALAS2 → ↓haem → ring sideroblasts); microcytic/hypochromic/dimorphic; ↑serum Fe, ↑ferritin, ↓TIBC
NeurologicalPeripheral neuropathy (sensory, axonal); ↓GABA → seizures (infants), hyperirritability, EEG changes
DermatologicalCheilosis, angular stomatitis, glossitis, seborrhoeic dermatitis (nasolabial, scrotal)
Metabolic↓Cystathionine β-synthase → ↑homocysteine; ↓kynureninase → ↑xanthurenic acid (tryptophan load test)

Diagnosis: Plasma PLP <20 nmol/L (ref 20–150); erythrocyte aspartate aminotransferase activity coefficient (EAST-AC) >1.8; urinary 4-pyridoxic acid <2 µmol/day; tryptophan load test ↑xanthurenic acid.

3. Section 3: Pyridoxine Toxicity

Sensory neuronopathy: Dose >100 mg/day chronic (typically >500 mg/day); large sensory fibre degeneration (dorsal columns); loss of vibration/position sense, sensory ataxia; reversible on stopping (months); motor spared.

Mechanism: PLP competes with GABA?; direct toxicity to dorsal root ganglia.

Treatment: Stop pyridoxine; symptomatic; recovery over 6–24 months.

4. Section 4: Biotin (B7) Metabolism

Sources: Liver, egg yolk, nuts, seeds, salmon, avocado, sweet potato; gut flora synthesis (significant contribution).

Absorption: Small intestine; SMVT (sodium-dependent multivitamin transporter, SLC5A6); also colonic absorption from flora.

Holocarboxylase synthetase (HLCS): Attaches biotin to lysine ε-amino group of apocarboxylases (ATP-dependent).

Biotinidase (BTD): Recycles biotin from biocytin (biotin-lysine) and dietary protein-bound biotin; serum biotinidase activity = diagnostic.

Carboxylases (4 mitochondrial):

  1. Acetyl-CoA Carboxylase (ACC): Acetyl-CoA + HCO₃⁻ → malonyl-CoA (fatty acid synthesis rate-limiting)
  2. Pyruvate Carboxylase (PC): Pyruvate + HCO₃⁻ → oxaloacetate (anaplerosis, gluconeogenesis)
  3. Propionyl-CoA Carboxylase (PCC): Propionyl-CoA → methylmalonyl-CoA (odd-chain FA, BCAA: Val, Ile, Met, Thr; cholesterol side-chain)
  4. Methylcrotonyl-CoA Carboxylase (MCC): Leucine → β-methylcrotonyl-CoA → β-methylglutaconyl-CoA

5. Section 5: Biotin Deficiency

Causes:

  • Raw egg whites: Avidin binds biotin (Kd 10⁻¹⁵ M); heat denatures avidin
  • TPN without biotin (historical; now added)
  • Prolonged broad-spectrum antibiotics → ↓gut flora synthesis
  • Biotinidase deficiency (BTD): AR; profound (<10%) or partial (10–30%); neonatal screening (many countries)
  • Holocarboxylase synthetase deficiency (HLCS): AR; neonatal onset; severe
  • Anticonvulsants (valproate, carbamazepine, phenytoin, phenobarbital) → ↑biotin catabolism
  • Pregnancy/lactation → ↑demand, marginal status common

Clinical Features:

  • Dermatitis: Scaly, erythematous, periorificial (eyes, nose, mouth, perineum); "biotin-deficient facies"
  • Alopecia: Total (universalis) or patchy
  • Conjunctivitis: Blepharoconjunctivitis
  • Neurological: Hypotonia, lethargy, seizures, ataxia, developmental delay (untreated children)
  • Metabolic: Lactic acidosis, organic aciduria (3-hydroxyisovaleric acid, methylcitrate), hyperammonaemia (↓PCC → propionyl-CoA accumulation)
  • Immunological: ↓T-cell function, candidiasis

Diagnosis: Serum biotinidase activity (profound <10%, partial 10–30%); urinary organic acids (↑3-hydroxyisovalerate, methylcitrate); plasma biotin (low); genetic testing BTD/HLCS.

6. Section 6: Pantothenic Acid (B5)

CoA synthesis: Pantothenate → 4'-phosphopantetheine → CoA (universal acyl carrier); ACP (fatty acid synthase).

Ubiquitous: "Pantos" = everywhere; deficiency virtually unknown in humans.

Experimental deficiency (burning feet syndrome): Paresthesia, burning feet, fatigue, headache, insomnia, GI distress; prisoner studies WWII.

Pantetheine / Pantethine: Used for dyslipidaemia (↓TG, ↑HDL); cystinosis (cysteamine depletion).

4. 4. Clinical Correlation

ScenarioActionNotes
45M on INH, peripheral neuropathy, sideroblastic anaemiaPyridoxine 50–100 mg/day; continue INHINH prophylaxis should have been given
6m infant, seizures, hypotonia, scaly periorificial rash, alopeciaBiotin 5–10 mg/day; genetic test BTD/HLCSBiotinidase deficiency; neonatal screen if available
30F, raw egg white "health shake" daily, dermatitis, alopeciaStop raw egg whites; biotin 10 mg/dayAvidin binds biotin; cooked eggs safe
60M on valproate, thinning hair, scaly rashBiotin 5 mg/day; review anticonvulsantValproate ↑biotin catabolism
40F, chronic pyridoxine 500 mg/day for "nerves", sensory ataxiaStop pyridoxine; monitor recovery 6–24 monthsSensory neuronopathy (>100 mg/day chronic)
30M, alcoholic, cheilosis, glossitis, sideroblastic anaemiaPyridoxine 50 mg/day + thiamine + folate; nutritional rehabMultiple deficiencies common

5. 5. High-Yield FCPS/MRCP Points

[!important]
- Must know: PLP cofactor for transamination, decarboxylation, glycogen phosphorylase, ALAS2 (haem), CBS (homocysteine); deficiency = sideroblastic anaemia, neuropathy, seizures, cheilosis; INH/penicillamine/hydralazine/OCP cause; toxicity = sensory neuronopathy >100 mg/day; Biotin → carboxylases (ACC, PC, PCC, MCC); deficiency = raw egg whites (avidin), antibiotics, biotinidase deficiency → dermatitis, alopecia, conjunctivitis, organic aciduria; Pantothenic acid = CoA/ACP; deficiency RARE (burning feet)
- Common viva: Sideroblastic anaemia mechanism (ALAS2); pyridoxine drugs (INH, penicillamine, hydralazine); pyridoxine toxicity vs deficiency; biotin carboxylases; avidin-raw egg white; biotinidase deficiency screening; burning feet syndrome = pantothenic acid
- Exam trap: Confusing pyridoxine toxicity (sensory neuronopathy) with deficiency (neuropathy); sideroblastic anaemia = microcytic but ↑Fe/ferritin; biotin deficiency vs zinc deficiency (both dermatitis/alopecia); pantothenic acid deficiency never seen clinically

6. 6. Common Confusions / Exam Traps

TrapCorrection
Pyridoxine toxicity = deficiency neuropathyToxicity = sensory neuronopathy (dorsal column, >100 mg/day); Deficiency = sensorimotor axonal neuropathy
Sideroblastic = iron deficiencySideroblastic: microcytic but ↑Fe, ↑ferritin, ring sideroblasts; Iron deficiency: ↓Fe, ↓ferritin
Biotin deficiency = zinc deficiencyBoth: dermatitis + alopecia; Biotin: conjunctivitis, organic aciduria, seizures; Zinc: diarrhoea, immune, taste loss
Pantothenic acid deficiency = commonEXTREMELY RARE; only experimental "burning feet syndrome"
Avidin in cooked eggsHeat denatures avidin; only RAW egg whites bind biotin
Biotinidase deficiency = HLCS deficiencyBTD: biotin recycling defect (later onset); HLCS: biotin attachment defect (neonatal, severe)
Pyridoxine for all neuropathiesOnly B6-responsive (sideroblastic, INH, deficiency); toxic at high dose

7. 7. Mnemonics

  • PLP enzymes: TD-GH-CK-AL = Transamination, Decarboxylation, Glycogen phosphorylase, Haem (ALAS2), CBS (homocysteine), Kynureninase, ALAS2, L (cystathionine)
  • Sideroblastic anaemia: SIDERO = Sideroblastic, Iron ↑, Dimorphic, Erythroid ALAS2, Ring sideroblasts, O (microcytic but ↑Fe)
  • Pyridoxine drugs: IPHOC = INH, Penicillamine, Hydralazine, Oral contraceptive, Cycloserine
  • Pyridoxine toxicity: TOXIC B6 = Toxic >100 mg/day → Sensory neuronopathy (dorsal column), Numbness, Ataxia, Reversible
  • Biotin carboxylases: APMM = Acetyl-CoA carboxylase, Pyruvate carboxylase, Propionyl-CoA carboxylase, Methylcrotonyl-CoA carboxylase
  • Avidin: RAW EGGS = Raw Egg Whites → Avidin binds Biotin
  • Biotin deficiency: DAC = Dermatitis (periorificial), Alopecia, Conjunctivitis (+ Organic aciduria)
  • Biotinidase vs HLCS: BTD = REcycle (later); HLCS = ATtach (neonatal)
  • Pantothenic acid: PANTOS = Pantothenic Acid = Everywhere (ubiquitous) → Burning feet only

8. 8. Mind Map

mindmap
  root((B6, Biotin, B5))
    Pyridoxine (B6)
      Forms: PN, PL, PM → PLP (active)
      PLP Enzymes
        Transaminases (ALT, AST, BCAT)
        Decarboxylases (DOPA, 5-HTP, GAD, His)
        Glycogen Phosphorylase (structural)
        Haem: ALAS2
        Homocysteine: CBS
        Tryptophan→Niacin: Kynureninase
      Deficiency
        Sideroblastic anaemia (ALAS2)
        Peripheral neuropathy
        Seizures (↓GABA)
        Cheilosis, glossitis
        Drugs: INH, penicillamine, hydralazine, OCP
      Toxicity
        Sensory neuronopathy >100 mg/day
        Dorsal column, reversible
    Biotin (B7)
      Carboxylases (APMM)
        Acetyl-CoA Carboxylase (FA synthesis)
        Pyruvate Carboxylase (gluconeogenesis)
        Propionyl-CoA Carboxylase (odd-chain FA, BCAA)
        Methylcrotonyl-CoA Carboxylase (Leu)
      Deficiency
        Raw egg whites (avidin)
        Antibiotics (gut flora)
        Biotinidase (BTD) / HLCS deficiency
        Anticonvulsants, pregnancy
      Features: DAC = Dermatitis (periorificial), Alopecia, Conjunctivitis
        + Organic aciduria (3-hydroxyisovalerate), seizures, metabolic acidosis
    Pantothenic Acid (B5)
      CoA (4'-phosphopantetheine) + ACP
      Ubiquitous
      Deficiency: Burning feet syndrome (rare)

9. 9. -Hour Recall Prompts

  1. PLP enzymes: transamination, decarboxylation, glycogen phosphorylase, ALAS2, CBS, kynureninase
  2. Sideroblastic anaemia: ring sideroblasts, ↑Fe/ferritin, microcytic but dimorphic
  3. Pyridoxine drugs: INH, penicillamine, hydralazine, OCP (IPHOC)
  4. Pyridoxine toxicity: sensory neuronopathy >100 mg/day (dorsal column)
  5. Biotin carboxylases: APMM (ACC, PC, PCC, MCC)
  6. Biotin deficiency: raw egg whites (avidin), antibiotics, BTD/HLCS
  7. Biotin features: DAC (dermatitis periorificial, alopecia, conjunctivitis) + organic aciduria
  8. Pantothenic acid = CoA/ACP; deficiency = burning feet syndrome (rare)

10. 10. -Day / 15-Day / 30-Day Revision Tracker

DayDateRecall Quality (1-5)Time SpentNotes
1
7
15
30

11. 11. Must Know / Should Know / Nice to Know

PriorityContent
Must Know 🔴PLP enzymes (transamination, decarboxylation, ALAS2, CBS, glycogen phosphorylase); sideroblastic anaemia; B6 deficiency causes (INH, penicillamine, hydralazine, OCP); pyridoxine toxicity (sensory neuronopathy >100 mg); Biotin carboxylases (APMM); biotin deficiency (raw egg, antibiotics, BTD/HLCS); pantothenic acid = CoA/ACP
Should Know 🟡Pyridoxine toxicity mechanism; biotinidase vs HLCS deficiency; organic aciduria in biotin deficiency; anticonvulsant-biotin interaction; pyridoxine in premenstrual syndrome/nausea; CoA structure
Nice to Know 🟢PLP-Schiff base mechanism detail; biotin in multiple carboxylase deficiency genetics; pantethine for dyslipidaemia/cystinosis; pyridoxine for morning sickness; B6 in carpal tunnel

12. 12. My Weak Points

  • [ ] Exact PLP-Schiff base catalytic mechanism for transamination
  • [ ] Biotinidase assay methodology
  • [ ] Pantethine clinical trial data for dyslipidaemia

13. 13. Self-Test Scorecard

DomainScore /10Target /10
Understanding8+
Recall8+
MCQ Performance8+
SBA Performance8+
Viva Confidence8+
TOTAL40+/50

14. 14. Exam Answer Modes

1. Long Answer / Essay (20 min)

Topic: "Pyridoxine, Biotin, and Pantothenic Acid: metabolism, deficiency, and toxicity"

  • Pyridoxine: PN/PL/PM → PLP; >140 enzymes (transaminases, decarboxylases, glycogen phosphorylase, ALAS2, CBS, kynureninase); deficiency: sideroblastic anaemia, neuropathy, seizures, cheilosis; drugs: INH, penicillamine, hydralazine, OCP; toxicity: sensory neuronopathy >100 mg/day
  • Biotin: carboxylase cofactor (ACC, PC, PCC, MCC); avidin (raw egg), antibiotics, BTD/HLCS deficiency; features: periorificial dermatitis, alopecia, conjunctivitis, organic aciduria, seizures
  • Pantothenic acid: CoA + ACP component; ubiquitous; deficiency only experimental (burning feet syndrome)

2. Short Note (7 min)

Topic: "Sideroblastic Anaemia in Pyridoxine Deficiency"

  • Mechanism: ALAS2 (δ-ALA synthase 2) is PLP-dependent; mitochondrial enzyme in erythroblasts; glycine + succinyl-CoA → δ-ALA (first step haem synthesis)
  • Deficiency: ↓PLP → ↓ALAS2 activity → ↓haem synthesis → iron accumulates in mitochondria → ring sideroblasts (Prussian blue +)
  • Blood picture: Microcytic hypochromic dimorphic (mixed microcytic + normocytic); ↑serum iron, ↑ferritin, ↑transferrin saturation, ↓TIBC
  • Causes: Pyridoxine deficiency (INH, alcohol), lead poisoning, myelodysplasia, alcohol, drugs (chloramphenicol)
  • Treatment: Pyridoxine 50–100 mg/day (pyridoxine-responsive); if unresponsive → transfusion support

3. Viva Answer (3 min)

Q: "Differentiate pyridoxine deficiency neuropathy from pyridoxine toxicity neuropathy."

"A: Deficiency: sensorimotor axonal neuropathy (distal, symmetrical, glove-stocking); Toxicity: pure sensory neuronopathy (dorsal column: loss vibration/position sense, sensory ataxia, pseudoathetosis); dose >100 mg/day chronic; toxicity reversible on stopping (6–24 months); motor spared in toxicity."

4. Ward Case Discussion (5 min)

Case: 35M on INH/RIF/PZA/EMB for TB, 3 months, presents with fatigue, pallor. Hb 8 g/dL, MCV 72, dimorphic blood film, ring sideroblasts on marrow, serum Fe 180 µg/dL, ferritin 800.

"Diagnosis: INH-induced pyridoxine deficiency → sideroblastic anaemia. Pyridoxine 100 mg/day (prophylaxis should have been 10–25 mg/day from start). Continue anti-TB drugs. Monitor Hb response (reticulocytosis 5–7 days, Hb rise 2–3 weeks). Check B6 level if available."

5. Last-Night-Before-Exam Sheet (1 min)

  • PLP enzymes: TD-GH-CK-AL = Transamination, Decarboxylation, Glycogen phosphorylase, Haem (ALAS2), Homocysteine (CBS), Kynureninase
  • Sideroblastic anaemia: Ring sideroblasts, ↑Fe/ferritin, microcytic but dimorphic; pyridoxine-responsive
  • B6 drugs: IPHOC = INH, Penicillamine, Hydralazine, OCP, Cycloserine
  • B6 toxicity: >100 mg/day → sensory neuronopathy (dorsal column, ataxia, reversible)
  • Biotin carboxylases: APMM = ACC, PC, PCC, MCC
  • Biotin deficiency: Raw egg (avidin), antibiotics, BTD/HLCS; DAC = Dermatitis (periorificial), Alopecia, Conjunctivitis + organic aciduria
  • Biotinidase vs HLCS: BTD = RECYCLE (later); HLCS = ATTACH (neonatal)
  • Pantothenic acid: CoA/ACP; deficiency = burning feet (rare)

15. 15. MCQs (10)

  1. Active cofactor form of vitamin B6:

A. Pyridoxine

B. Pyridoxal

C. Pyridoxal 5'-phosphate (PLP)

D. Pyridoxamine

  1. PLP-dependent enzyme for haem synthesis:

A. Ferrochelatase

B. ALAS2 (δ-ALA synthase 2)

C. Porphobilinogen deaminase

D. Uroporphyrinogen decarboxylase

  1. Sideroblastic anaemia iron studies:

A. Low Fe, low ferritin, high TIBC

B. High Fe, high ferritin, low TIBC

C. Normal Fe, high ferritin, normal TIBC

D. High Fe, low ferritin, high TIBC

  1. Drug causing pyridoxine deficiency by forming hydrazone with PLP:

A. Methotrexate

B. Isoniazid

C. Trimethoprim

D. Ciprofloxacin

  1. Pyridoxine toxicity (>100 mg/day chronic) causes:

A. Motor neuronopathy

B. Sensory neuronopathy (dorsal column)

C. Autonomic neuropathy

D. Cranial nerve palsies

  1. Biotin carboxylases (APMM):

A. Acetyl-CoA carboxylase, Pyruvate carboxylase, Propionyl-CoA carboxylase, Methylcrotonyl-CoA carboxylase

B. Acetyl-CoA carboxylase, Pyruvate dehydrogenase, Propionyl-CoA carboxylase, Methylmalonyl-CoA mutase

C. ATP-citrate lyase, Pyruvate carboxylase, Propionyl-CoA carboxylase, Methylcrotonyl-CoA carboxylase

D. Acetyl-CoA carboxylase, Phosphoenolpyruvate carboxylase, Propionyl-CoA carboxylase, Methylcrotonyl-CoA carboxylase

  1. Raw egg white binds biotin via:

A. Albumin

B. Avidin

C. Ovalbumin

D. Conalbumin

  1. Biotinidase deficiency vs Holocarboxylase synthetase deficiency:

A. BTD = neonatal onset, severe; HLCS = later onset

B. BTD = recycling defect (later onset); HLCS = attachment defect (neonatal, severe)

C. Both neonatal onset

D. Both respond to low-dose biotin

  1. Biotin deficiency clinical triad (DAC):

A. Diarrhoea, Anaemia, Convulsions

B. Dermatitis (periorificial), Alopecia, Conjunctivitis

C. Dizziness, Ataxia, Confusion

D. Diarrhoea, Alopecia, Cheilosis

  1. Pantothenic acid deficiency syndrome:

A. Pellagra

B. Burning feet syndrome

C. Beriberi

D. Scurvy

16. 16. SBA Questions (5)

  1. A 40-year-old man on isoniazid for TB presents with anaemia (Hb 8.5, MCV 70), dimorphic blood film, ring sideroblasts on marrow, serum iron 200 µg/dL. Best management?

A. Stop INH, switch to alternative TB regimen

B. Pyridoxine 100 mg/day; continue INH

C. Iron supplementation

D. Blood transfusion only

E. Pyridoxine only if anaemia worsens

  1. A 6-month-old infant on TPN without biotin develops scaly periorificial rash, alopecia, conjunctivitis, and metabolic acidosis. Urinary organic acids show elevated 3-hydroxyisovaleric acid. Diagnosis?

A. Zinc deficiency

B. Biotin deficiency

C. Essential fatty acid deficiency

D. Multiple carboxylase deficiency (genetic)

E. Zinc + biotin deficiency

  1. A 30-year-old woman takes pyridoxine 500 mg daily for "premenstrual syndrome" for 2 years. She develops progressive sensory ataxia, loss of vibration sense, and pseudoathetosis. Nerve conduction studies show absent sensory potentials, normal motor. Diagnosis?

A. Guillain-Barré syndrome

B. Pyridoxine toxicity (sensory neuronopathy)

C. Vitamin B12 deficiency

D. Syringomyelia

E. Multiple sclerosis

  1. A mother of a 4-month-old exclusively breastfed infant consumes 6 raw egg whites daily in "protein shakes." Infant develops scaly rash around eyes/mouth, alopecia, and conjunctivitis. Cause?

A. Zinc deficiency

B. Biotin deficiency (avidin in raw egg whites via breast milk)

C. Essential fatty acid deficiency

D. Cow's milk protein allergy

E. Seborrhoeic dermatitis

  1. A 55-year-old alcoholic presents with burning feet, fatigue, and insomnia. No neurological deficits. Dietary history reveals minimal intake of all food groups. Which vitamin deficiency is classically described but virtually never seen in isolation?

A. Thiamine

B. Pantothenic acid (burning feet syndrome)

C. Niacin

D. Riboflavin

E. Vitamin B12

17. 17. Flashcards

  • Q: PLP =

A: Pyridoxal 5'-phosphate (active B6 cofactor)

  • Q: PLP enzymes mnemonic

A: TD-GH-CK-AL = Transamination, Decarboxylation, Glycogen phosphorylase, Haem (ALAS2), Homocysteine (CBS), Kynureninase

  • Q: Sideroblastic anaemia lab

A: Microcytic, ↑Fe, ↑ferritin, ring sideroblasts, dimorphic picture

  • Q: B6 drugs

A: IPHOC = INH, Penicillamine, Hydralazine, OCP, Cycloserine

  • Q: B6 toxicity

A: Sensory neuronopathy >100 mg/day (dorsal column, reversible)

  • Q: Biotin carboxylases

A: APMM = Acetyl-CoA carboxylase, Pyruvate carboxylase, Propionyl-CoA carboxylase, Methylcrotonyl-CoA carboxylase

  • Q: Avidin

A: Raw egg white glycoprotein; binds biotin 10¹⁵ affinity; heat denatures

  • Q: Biotin deficiency features

A: DAC = Dermatitis (periorificial), Alopecia, Conjunctivitis (+ organic aciduria)

  • Q: BTD vs HLCS

A: BTD = RECYCLE (later); HLCS = ATTACH (neonatal, severe)

  • Q: 3-hydroxyisovalerate

A: Biotin deficiency marker (MCC substrate accumulation)

  • Q: Pantothenic acid

A: CoA + ACP component; ubiquitous; deficiency = burning feet (rare)

18. 18. Answer Key with Explanations

1. MCQs

  1. C — PLP is the catalytically active coenzyme form.
  2. B — ALAS2 (erythroid δ-ALA synthase) requires PLP; first step of haem synthesis.
  3. B — Sideroblastic anaemia: iron overload (↑Fe, ↑ferritin, ↓TIBC) due to impaired haem synthesis → mitochondrial iron accumulation.
  4. B — INH forms hydrazone with PLP → increased renal excretion; also inhibits pyridoxal kinase.
  5. B — Chronic high-dose pyridoxine (>100 mg/day) → sensory neuronopathy (dorsal column degeneration); motor spared; reversible on cessation.
  6. A — APMM: Acetyl-CoA carboxylase (FA synthesis), Pyruvate carboxylase (gluconeogenesis/anaplerosis), Propionyl-CoA carboxylase (odd-chain FA/BCAA), Methylcrotonyl-CoA carboxylase (leucine catabolism).
  7. B — Avidin (raw egg white) binds biotin with extremely high affinity (Kd 10⁻¹⁵ M); cooking denatures avidin.
  8. B — BTD (biotinidase) recycles biotin from biocytin/dietary protein (later onset); HLCS attaches biotin to apocarboxylases (neonatal, severe).
  9. B — DAC: Dermatitis (periorificial scaly), Alopecia (universalis), Conjunctivitis.
  10. B — Pantothenic acid experimental deficiency = burning feet syndrome (paresthesia, dysesthesia); virtually never seen in normal populations.

2. SBAs

  1. B — INH-induced sideroblastic anaemia: pyridoxine 100 mg/day reverses; prophylaxis 10–25 mg/day should be routine; continue INH.
  2. B — Biotin deficiency: scaly periorificial dermatitis, alopecia, conjunctivitis, metabolic acidosis, ↑3-hydroxyisovalerate (MCC substrate); TPN without biotin or raw egg whites.
  3. B — Pyridoxine toxicity: sensory neuronopathy (large fibre dorsal column) at doses >100 mg/day chronic; absent sensory NCS, normal motor; reversible on stopping.
  4. B — Avidin in raw egg whites consumed by mother → secreted in breast milk → infant biotin deficiency; maternal biotin supplementation also helps.
  5. B — Pantothenic acid deficiency = "burning feet syndrome" (experimental, WWII prisoners); virtually never seen in clinical practice; ubiquitous in foods.

19. 19. Summary

Pyridoxine (B6), Biotin (B7) & Pantothenic Acid (B5) are Must Know 🔴 topics for FCPS/MRCP.

Key takeaway: Pyridoxine → PLP (transamination, decarboxylation, glycogen phosphorylase, ALAS2, CBS, kynureninase); deficiency = sideroblastic anaemia, neuropathy, seizures, cheilosis; drugs (INH, penicillamine, hydralazine, OCP) cause deficiency; toxicity = sensory neuronopathy >100 mg/day. Biotin → carboxylases (ACC, PC, PCC, MCC); deficiency: raw egg whites (avidin), antibiotics, BTD/HLCS → periorificial dermatitis, alopecia, conjunctivitis, organic aciduria. Pantothenic acid = CoA + ACP; ubiquitous; deficiency virtually non-existent (burning feet syndrome).

Exam focus: Sideroblastic anaemia mechanism, B6 drug interactions, pyridoxine toxicity vs deficiency, biotin carboxylases (APMM), avidin, biotinidase vs HLCS, DAC features.

Clinical relevance: INH prophylaxis mandatory; TPN biotin supplementation; anticonvulsant-biotin monitoring; pyridoxine dosing safety.

Template version: 1.0 | Davidson 24e Ch 22 aligned | FCPS/MRCP oriented

topics.title: Riboflavin B2 Niacin B3 Pellagra
topics.html:
Related: [[Nutritional Factors in Disease MOC]], [[Davidson Chapter 22 - Nutritional Factors in Disease Hierarchy]], [[../00_Index/Medicine MOC|Medicine MOC]]

[!important]
Riboflavin (B2) = FMN/FAD cofactor for oxidoreductases; deficiency = cheilosis, angular stomatitis, glossitis, corneal vascularisation; Niacin (B3) = NAD/NADP; deficiency = pellagra (3 D's: dermatitis, diarrhoea, dementia); tryptophan → niacin (60:1).

1. 1. Learning Objectives

  • [ ] Describe riboflavin → FMN/FAD; flavoprotein enzymes (ETC Complex I/II, MTHFR, glutathione reductase, xanthine oxidase)
  • [ ] List riboflavin deficiency signs: cheilosis, angular stomatitis, magenta glossitis, corneal vascularisation, normocytic anaemia
  • [ ] Explain niacin (nicotinic acid/nicotinamide) → NAD/NADP; 400+ dehydrogenase reactions
  • [ ] Detail pellagra: dermatitis (Casal's necklace), diarrhoea, dementia, death; tryptophan → niacin (60 mg tryptophan = 1 mg niacin)
  • [ ] Identify Hartnup disease (neutral AA transporter defect → tryptophan malabsorption → pellagra)
  • [ ] State carcinoid syndrome → tryptophan → serotonin → niacin deficiency
  • [ ] Know isoniazid → pyridoxine/B3 deficiency; treatment: nicotinamide (no flush)

2. 2. Definitions / Key Concepts

TermDefinition
Riboflavin (B2)Flavin mononucleotide (FMN) + flavin adenine dinucleotide (FAD); yellow fluorescent
FMNRiboflavin-5'-phosphate; 1-electron transfers; Complex I (NADH dehydrogenase)
FADFMN + ATP; 2-electron transfers; Complex II (succinate dehydrogenase), acyl-CoA dehydrogenase, MTHFR
Glutathione ReductaseFAD-dependent; regenerates GSH from GSSG; riboflavin deficiency → ↓GSH → oxidative stress
MTHFRFAD-dependent; 5,10-methylene-THF → 5-methyl-THF (homocysteine remethylation)
CheilosisFissures at corners of mouth (angular stomatitis); riboflavin deficiency
Magenta GlossitisSmooth, beefy red, swollen tongue; atrophy of papillae; B2/B3/B12 deficiency
Corneal VascularisationSuperficial neovascularisation of cornea; pathognomonic riboflavin deficiency
Niacin (B3)Nicotinic acid + nicotinamide; precursor of NAD⁺/NADP⁺; diet + tryptophan (60:1)
NAD⁺/NADHOxidised/reduced; catabolic dehydrogenases (glycolysis, Krebs, β-oxidation)
NADP⁺/NADPHAnabolic (fatty acid synthesis, glutathione reductase, PPP)
Pellagra3 D's: Dermatitis, Diarrhoea, Dementia (+ Death); photosensitivity rash
Casal's NecklaceSymmetrical hyperpigmented rash on sun-exposed neck/Casal area; pellagra hallmark
Tryptophan → Niacin60 mg tryptophan → 1 mg niacin; requires B2 (FAD), B6 (PLP), Fe; kynurenine pathway
Hartnup DiseaseSLC6A19 mutation → neutral AA malabsorption (tryptophan) → pellagra-like rash, cerebellar ataxia
Carcinoid SyndromeSerotonin-secreting NET → tryptophan → serotonin (not niacin) → pellagra
Isoniazid (INH)Anti-TB; hydrazine → inhibits pyridoxal kinase → B6 deficiency AND kynureninase → niacin deficiency
Niacin FlushNicotinic acid → prostaglandin D2 → cutaneous vasodilation, pruritus; absent with nicotinamide
NAD⁺ PrecursorsNicotinamide riboside (NR), nicotinamide mononucleotide (NMN) — anti-ageing research

3. 3. Core Content

1. Section 1: Riboflavin (B2) Metabolism

Sources: Milk, eggs, liver, green veg, fortified cereals; light-sensitive (opaque containers).

Absorption: Small intestine; carrier-mediated (RFVT1/SLC52A1, RFVT2/SLC52A2, RFVT3/SLC52A3); phosphorylation to FMN → FAD.

Cofactors: FMN (Complex I), FAD (Complex II, acyl-CoA dehydrogenase, MTHFR, glutathione reductase, xanthine oxidase, monoamine oxidase).

Deficiency (ariboflavinosis): Rare isolated; usually with other B-vitamin deficiencies.

Features: Cheilosis, angular stomatitis, magenta glossitis, corneal vascularisation (superficial neovascularisation), seborrhoeic dermatitis (nasolabial, scrotal/vulval), normocytic normochromic anaemia (↓erythropoietin), peripheral neuropathy.

At risk: Alcohol, elderly, anorexia, phototherapy (bilirubin degradation), vegans (no dairy).

Diagnosis: Erythrocyte glutathione reductase activity coefficient (EGRAC) >1.2–1.4; urinary riboflavin <27 µg/g creatinine.

Treatment: Riboflavin 10–30 mg/day oral; multivitamin if multiple deficiencies.

2. Section 2: Niacin (B3) Metabolism

Forms: Nicotinic acid (niacin), nicotinamide (niacinamide); both → NAD/NAD.

Dietary sources: Meat, fish, poultry, peanuts, legumes, fortified grains; coffee (high).

Tryptophan pathway: 60 mg tryptophan → 1 mg niacin (via kynurenine pathway); requires B2 (FAD for kynurenine 3-monooxygenase), B6 (PLP for kynureninase), Fe.

NAD⁺ synthesis pathways:

  1. Preiss-Handler: Nicotinic acid → NaMN → NaAD → NAD⁺
  2. Salvage: Nicotinamide → NMN → NAD⁺
  3. De novo: Tryptophan → kynurenine → 3-hydroxykynurenine → 3-hydroxyanthranilate → quinolinate → NAD⁺

Functions: NAD⁺ (catabolism: glycolysis, PDH, Krebs, β-oxidation, ETC); NADPH (anabolism: fatty acid/cholesterol synthesis, PPP, glutathione reductase, CYP450).

3. Section 3: Pellagra — Clinical Features

DFeatureDetails
DermatitisPhotosensitive rashSymmetrical, sun-exposed (face, neck-Casal's necklace, hands-glove, feet-boot); hyperpigmented, scaling, vesiculobullous → hyperkeratotic
DiarrhoeaGI involvementWatery, sometimes bloody; glossitis, stomatitis, gastritis, malabsorption
DementiaNeuropsychiatricConfusion, memory loss, disorientation, depression, hallucinations, psychosis; reversible if early
DeathUntreatedMulti-organ failure; cardiovascular collapse

Pellagrous dermatosis: "Casal's necklace" (broad collar rash on neck), "gauntlet" rash on forearms, "boot" rash on legs; sharply demarcated at衣领/ sleeve lines.

4. Section 4: Secondary Niacin Deficiency

CauseMechanism
Hartnup DiseaseSLC6A19 (B⁰AT1) mutation → neutral AA malabsorption (tryptophan) → ↓niacin synthesis; cerebellar ataxia, nystagmus, pellagra rash; autosomal recessive
Carcinoid SyndromeSerotonin-secreting NET → tryptophan → 5-HTP → serotonin (via TPH, AADC) → ↑5-HIAA; tryptophan diverted from niacin pathway
Isoniazid (INH)Hydrazine metabolite → inhibits pyridoxal kinase (B6) → ↓PLP → kynureninase inhibition → ↑xanthurenic acid, ↓niacin; give pyridoxine 10–25 mg/day prophylaxis
Carcinoid + INHDual hit → severe pellagra
Malnutrition / Alcohol↓Intake + ↓tryptophan → niacin
Carcinoid + HartnupRare combination

5. Section 5: Diagnosis & Treatment

Diagnosis: Urinary N¹-methylnicotinamide (NMN) or N¹-methyl-2-pyridone-5-carboxamide (2-PYR) excretion <0.8 mg/day; plasma NAD⁺; clinical response to niacin.

Treatment:

  • Nicotinamide 300 mg/day PO (divided doses) — preferred (no flush)
  • Nicotinic acid 100–300 mg/day — causes flush (prostaglandin D2); aspirin 30 min prior reduces
  • Hartnup: High-protein diet + nicotinamide 50–100 mg/day
  • Carcinoid: Nicotinamide 300 mg/day + treat NET; tryptophan supplementation controversial
  • INH prophylaxis: Pyridoxine 10–25 mg/day (prevents B6 + B3 deficiency)

6. Section 6: Niacin Toxicity & Therapeutic Use

Therapeutic (lipid-lowering): Nicotinic acid 1.5–3 g/day → ↓LDL 10–20%, ↓TG 20–50%, ↑HDL 15–35%; flushing (PGD2 via GPER109A on Langerhans cells); laropiprant (PGD2 receptor antagonist) withdrawn.

Adverse: Hepatotoxicity (time-release > immediate-release), hyperglycaemia, hyperuricaemia (↓renal urate excretion), GI upset, hypotension.

Nicotinamide: No flush, no lipid effect, safe in high dose; used for pellagra, actinic keratosis prevention, osteoarthritis (cartilage).

NAD⁺ boosters: Nicotinamide riboside (NR), NMN — anti-ageing, sirtuin activation; research phase.

4. 4. Clinical Correlation

ScenarioActionNotes
45M, alcohol, photosensitive neck rash, diarrhoea, confusionNicotinamide 300 mg/day PO; thiamine, folate, multivitaminPellagra; screen for alcohol withdrawal
8y child, episodic cerebellar ataxia, pellagra rash, neutral aminoaciduriaGenetic test SLC6A19 → Hartnup; high-protein diet + nicotinamide 100 mg/dayAutosomal recessive; tryptophan malabsorption
55F, flushing diarrhoea, carcinoid syndrome, pellagra rashNicotinamide 300 mg/day; somatostatin analogue (octreotide) for carcinoidTryptophan diverted to serotonin
30M, TB on INH, peripheral neuropathy, glossitisPyridoxine 25 mg/day prophylaxis; if pellagra → nicotinamide 300 mg/dayINH → B6 + B3 deficiency
70F, vegan, cheilosis, magenta tongue, corneal vascularisationRiboflavin 10 mg/day + B-complex; dietary counsellingMultiple B-vitamin deficiency

5. 5. High-Yield FCPS/MRCP Points

[!important]
- Must know: Riboflavin → FMN/FAD; deficiency = cheilosis, angular stomatitis, magenta glossitis, corneal vascularisation; Niacin = NAD/NADP; pellagra = 3 D's (dermatitis Casal's necklace, diarrhoea, dementia); tryptophan → niacin 60:1; Hartnup (SLC6A19, neutral AA); carcinoid diverts tryptophan; INH causes B6+B3 deficiency; nicotinamide for treatment (no flush)
- Common viva: 3 D's of pellagra; Casal's necklace; tryptophan → niacin pathway (B2, B6, Fe required); Hartnup vs pellagra; why nicotinamide not nicotinic acid for treatment; INH prophylaxis; riboflavin corneal vascularisation
- Exam trap: Confusing riboflavin (corneal vascularisation) with B12 (optic neuropathy); thinking niacin flush = allergy; giving nicotinic acid for pellagra (flush confuses); missing Hartnup (cerebellar ataxia + rash); tryptophan 60:1 ratio

6. 6. Common Confusions / Exam Traps

TrapCorrection
Niacin = nicotinamide for lipidsOnly nicotinic acid lowers lipids; nicotinamide = no flush, no lipid effect
Pellagra rash = photosensitive eczemaSharply demarcated at clothing lines (Casal's necklace, glove, boot)
Tryptophan → niacin always worksNeeds B2 (FAD), B6 (PLP), Fe; Hartnup = no tryptophan absorption
Corneal vascularisation = B12 deficiencyRiboflavin deficiency = superficial corneal vascularisation; B12 = optic neuropathy
INH only causes B6 deficiencyAlso causes niacin deficiency (kynureninase inhibition via B6 depletion)
Carcinoid diarrhoea = pellagra diarrhoeaCarcinoid: secretory (5-HT); Pellagra: inflammatory (glossitis, gastritis)
Hartnup = cystinuriaHartnup: neutral AA transporter (SLC6A19); Cystinuria: dibasic AA (SLC3A1/SLC7A9)
Niacin flush = anaphylaxisProstaglandin D2-mediated; aspirin 325 mg 30 min prior prevents

7. 7. Mnemonics

  • Riboflavin: FMN/FAD = Flavin Mono/Adenine Dinucleotide; Complex I (FMN), II (FAD)
  • Riboflavin deficiency: Cheilosis, Corneal vascularisation, Commissures (angular stomatitis), Glossitis (magenta)
  • Niacin: NAD/NADP = Nicotinamide Adenine Dinucleotide / Phosphate
  • Pellagra 3 D's: DDD = Dermatitis, Diarrhoea, Dementia (+ Death)
  • Casal's Necklace: Collar rash on Casal (neck) — photosensitive
  • Tryptophan → Niacin: 60:1 (60 mg tryptophan → 1 mg niacin); needs B2, B6, Fe
  • Hartnup: Hartnup = H (neutral amino Acid transporter SLC6A19); cerebellar Ataxia
  • Carcinoid: Carcinoid → Serotonin → Tryptophan diverted → Pellagra
  • INH: INH → Pyridoxine (B6) deficiency → Kynureninase inhibited → Niacin deficiency
  • Nicotinic acid vs nicotinamide: NICOTINIC = FLUSH + LIPIDS; NICOTINAMIDE = NO FLUSH, NO LIPIDS

8. 8. Mind Map

mindmap
  root((Riboflavin & Niacin))
    Riboflavin (B2)
      Metabolism
        Sources: Milk, eggs, liver
        Absorption: RFVT1/2/3 (SLC52A)
        FMN (Complex I)
        FAD (Complex II, MTHFR, GSH reductase)
      Deficiency (Ariboflavinosis)
        Cheilosis, angular stomatitis
        Magenta glossitis
        Corneal vascularisation
        Normocytic anaemia
      Diagnosis: EGRAC >1.2-1.4
      Treatment: 10-30 mg/day
    Niacin (B3)
      Forms
        Nicotinic acid (flush, lipids)
        Nicotinamide (no flush, pellagra Rx)
      Synthesis
        Dietary
        Tryptophan 60:1 (B2, B6, Fe)
        Kynurenine pathway
      NAD/NADP
        NAD: catabolism (glycolysis, Krebs, ETC)
        NADPH: anabolism (FA synthesis, PPP, GSH)
      Pellagra (3 D's)
        Dermatitis: Casal's necklace, glove, boot
        Diarrhoea: glossitis, gastritis
        Dementia: confusion, psychosis
      Secondary Causes
        Hartnup (SLC6A19, neutral AA)
        Carcinoid (tryptophan → serotonin)
        INH (B6 → kynureninase)
      Treatment
        Nicotinamide 300 mg/day (NO FLUSH)
        Hartnup: high protein + nicotinamide
        INH prophylaxis: Pyridoxine 10-25 mg/day
    Toxicity
      Nicotinic acid: flush (PGD2), hepatotoxicity, hyperglycaemia
      Nicotinamide: safe, no lipid effect

9. 9. -Hour Recall Prompts

  1. Riboflavin cofactors: FMN (Complex I), FAD (Complex II, MTHFR, GSH reductase)
  2. Riboflavin deficiency: cheilosis, angular stomatitis, magenta glossitis, corneal vascularisation
  3. Niacin → NAD/NADP; tryptophan 60:1 (needs B2, B6, Fe)
  4. Pellagra 3 D's: Dermatitis (Casal's necklace), Diarrhoea, Dementia, Death
  5. Hartnup: SLC6A19 neutral AA malabsorption → tryptophan → pellagra + cerebellar ataxia
  6. Carcinoid: tryptophan → serotonin → niacin deficiency
  7. INH: pyridoxine prophylaxis 10-25 mg/day prevents B6 + B3 deficiency
  8. Treatment: Nicotinamide 300 mg/day (no flush); nicotinic acid for lipids (flush)

10. 10. -Day / 15-Day / 30-Day Revision Tracker

DayDateRecall Quality (1-5)Time SpentNotes
1
7
15
30

11. 11. Must Know / Should Know / Nice to Know

PriorityContent
Must Know 🔴Riboflavin → FMN/FAD; deficiency signs (cheilosis, corneal vascularisation, magenta glossitis); Niacin → NAD/NADP; Pellagra 3 D's (Casal's necklace); Tryptophan 60:1 (B2, B6, Fe); Hartnup (SLC6A19); Carcinoid tryptophan diversion; INH prophylaxis; Nicotinamide for pellagra
Should Know 🟡EGRAC diagnosis; NAD⁺ precursor pathways (Preiss-Handler, salvage, de novo); Nicotinic acid lipid effects (+ flush); Carcinoid pathogenesis; Hartnup cerebellar ataxia; Niacin toxicity (hepatotoxicity time-release)
Nice to Know 🟢NR/NMN NAD⁺ boosters; riboflavin phototherapy interaction; riboflavin in migraine prophylaxis; niacin in schizoaffective; pharmacogenomics of niacin flush

12. 12. My Weak Points

  • [ ] Exact kynurenine pathway intermediates for tryptophan → niacin
  • [ ] MTHFR FAD-dependence structural basis
  • [ ] Laropiprant withdrawal reasons

13. 13. Self-Test Scorecard

DomainScore /10Target /10
Understanding8+
Recall8+
MCQ Performance8+
SBA Performance8+
Viva Confidence8+
TOTAL40+/50

14. 14. Exam Answer Modes

1. Long Answer / Essay (20 min)

Topic: "Riboflavin and Niacin: metabolism, deficiency syndromes, and clinical management"

  • Riboflavin: FMN (Complex I), FAD (Complex II, MTHFR, glutathione reductase); deficiency: cheilosis, angular stomatitis, magenta glossitis, corneal vascularisation, anaemia
  • Niacin: NAD/NADP; tryptophan 60:1 (requires B2, B6, Fe); pellagra: 3 D's (dermatitis Casal's necklace, diarrhoea, dementia); Casal's necklace hallmark
  • Secondary: Hartnup (SLC6A19, neutral AA, cerebellar ataxia), carcinoid (tryptophan → serotonin), INH (kynureninase inhibition via B6 depletion)
  • Treatment: Nicotinamide 300 mg/day (no flush); nicotinic acid for dyslipidaemia (flush); INH prophylaxis pyridoxine 10–25 mg/day

2. Short Note (7 min)

Topic: "Pellagra"

  • 3 D's: Dermatitis (photosensitive, Casal's necklace, glove/boot distribution), Diarrhoea (glossitis, gastritis), Dementia (confusion, psychosis), Death if untreated
  • Pathophysiology: Niacin deficiency → ↓NAD/NADP → impaired cellular respiration
  • Secondary causes: Hartnup (tryptophan malabsorption), carcinoid (tryptophan → serotonin), INH (B6 depletion → kynureninase inhibition), malnutrition
  • Treatment: Nicotinamide 300 mg/day PO divided (no flush); dietary niacin/tryptophan; treat underlying cause

3. Viva Answer (3 min)

Q: "Differentiate Hartnup disease from pellagra."

"A: Hartnup = genetic neutral AA transporter defect (SLC6A19) → tryptophan malabsorption → secondary pellagra + cerebellar ataxia, nystagmus, nystagmus. Pellagra = niacin deficiency (dietary or secondary) → 3 D's without cerebellar signs. Hartnup: autosomal recessive, high-protein diet + nicotinamide; Pellagra: treat cause + nicotinamide."

4. Ward Case Discussion (5 min)

Case: 50M, TB on INH/RIF/PZA/EMB 2 months, presents with burning feet, confusion, photosensitive rash on neck/hands.

"INH → pyridoxal kinase inhibition → B6 deficiency → kynureninase inhibition → niacin deficiency (pellagra). Immediate: Pyridoxine 25 mg/day + Nicotinamide 300 mg/day PO. Continue TB drugs. Monitor neuropathy resolution. Counsel: pyridoxine prophylaxis 10–25 mg/day should have been given from start."

5. Last-Night-Before-Exam Sheet (1 min)

  • Riboflavin: FMN (Complex I), FAD (Complex II, MTHFR, GSH reductase); deficiency: C-4: Cheilosis, Corneal vascularisation, Commissures (angular stomatitis), Glossitis (magenta)
  • Niacin: NAD/NADP; tryptophan 60:1 (needs B2, B6, Fe)
  • Pellagra: DDD = Dermatitis (Casal's necklace), Diarrhoea, Dementia, Death
  • Hartnup: SLC6A19 neutral AA → tryptophan malabsorption → pellagra + cerebellar ataxia
  • Carcinoid: Tryptophan → serotonin (5-HT) → ↑5-HIAA → niacin deficiency
  • INH: Pyridoxine 10–25 mg/day prophylaxis (prevents B6 + B3 deficiency)
  • Treatment: Nicotinamide 300 mg/day (NO FLUSH); Nicotinic acid for lipids (FLUSH)

15. 15. MCQs (10)

  1. Riboflavin cofactor for Complex I:

A. FAD

B. FMN

C. NAD⁺

D. CoQ

  1. Pathognomonic ocular sign of riboflavin deficiency:

A. Optic neuritis

B. Corneal vascularisation

C. Night blindness

D. Kayser-Fleischer ring

  1. Niacin synthesis from tryptophan ratio:

A. 10:1

B. 30:1

C. 60:1

D. 100:1

  1. Casal's necklace is seen in:

A. Riboflavin deficiency

B. Pellagra (niacin deficiency)

C. Vitamin B12 deficiency

D. Vitamin C deficiency

  1. Hartnup disease transporter defect:

A. SLC7A9 (dibasic AA)

B. SLC6A19 (neutral AA)

C. SLC3A1 (cystine)

D. SLC22A5 (carnitine)

  1. Carcinoid syndrome causes niacin deficiency by:

A. Inhibiting kynureninase

B. Diverting tryptophan to serotonin

C. Inhibiting tryptophan absorption

D. Increasing niacin excretion

  1. Isoniazid causes niacin deficiency via:

A. Direct inhibition of NAD synthesis

B. Pyridoxal kinase inhibition → B6 deficiency → kynureninase inhibition

C. Inhibiting tryptophan absorption

D. Increasing niacin catabolism

  1. Preferred treatment for pellagra (no flush):

A. Nicotinic acid 300 mg/day

B. Nicotinamide 300 mg/day

C. Tryptophan 2 g/day

C. Niacin extended-release

  1. Pyridoxine prophylaxis dose for INH:

A. 50–100 mg/day

B. 10–25 mg/day

C. 1–5 mg/day

D. 100–200 mg/day

  1. Nicotinic acid vs nicotinamide:

A. Both cause flush

B. Nicotinic acid = flush + lipid lowering; Nicotinamide = no flush, no lipid effect

C. Nicotinamide = flush + lipid lowering

D. Neither affects lipids

16. 16. SBA Questions (5)

  1. A 12-year-old boy presents with episodic cerebellar ataxia, nystagmus, and a photosensitive rash on sun-exposed areas. Urinary neutral amino acids increased. Diagnosis?

A. Pellagra

B. Hartnup disease

C. Carcinoid syndrome

D. Vitamin E deficiency

E. Acute intermittent porphyria

  1. A 55-year-old man with metastatic carcinoid syndrome (flushing, diarrhoea) develops a photosensitive rash on his neck and hands, glossitis, and confusion. Mechanism of niacin deficiency?

A. Impaired intestinal absorption of niacin

B. Tryptophan diverted to serotonin synthesis

C. Increased renal excretion of NAD precursors

D. Carcinoid secretion of niacinase

E. Inhibition of kynurenine 3-monooxygenase

  1. A 30-year-old man on isoniazid for pulmonary TB develops peripheral neuropathy and a photosensitive rash. Prevention strategy?

A. Increase INH dose

B. Pyridoxine 10–25 mg/day from initiation

C. Nicotinamide 100 mg/day

D. Vitamin B12 supplementation

E. Discontinue INH

  1. An alcoholic patient presents with cheilosis, angular stomatitis, magenta glossitis, and superficial corneal vascularisation. Most likely deficiency?

A. Thiamine

B. Riboflavin (B2)

C. Niacin

D. Vitamin B12

E. Folate

  1. A patient with pellagra is started on nicotinic acid 500 mg TDS. He develops intense facial flushing and pruritus. Prophylaxis for flushing?

A. H1 antihistamine

B. Aspirin 325 mg 30 min before dose

C. Corticosteroid

D. Switch to nicotinamide

E. Reduce dose to 100 mg

17. 17. Flashcards

  • Q: Riboflavin → cofactors

A: FMN (Complex I), FAD (Complex II, MTHFR, glutathione reductase, acyl-CoA dehydrogenase)

  • Q: Riboflavin deficiency eye sign

A: Corneal vascularisation (superficial neovascularisation)

  • Q: Riboflavin deficiency oral signs

A: Cheilosis, angular stomatitis, magenta glossitis

  • Q: Niacin coenzymes

A: NAD⁺/NADH (catabolism), NADP⁺/NADPH (anabolism)

  • Q: Tryptophan → niacin

A: 60 mg tryptophan = 1 mg niacin; needs B2 (FAD), B6 (PLP), Fe

  • Q: Pellagra 3 D's

A: Dermatitis (Casal's necklace), Diarrhoea, Dementia, Death

  • Q: Casal's necklace

A: Symmetrical hyperpigmented rash on neck (sun-exposed collar area)

  • Q: Hartnup disease

A: SLC6A19 neutral AA transporter → tryptophan malabsorption → pellagra + cerebellar ataxia; AR

  • Q: Carcinoid niacin deficiency

A: Tryptophan → serotonin (via TPH) → diverted from niacin pathway → pellagra

  • Q: INH niacin deficiency

A: INH → pyridoxal kinase inhibition → B6 deficiency → kynureninase inhibition → niacin deficiency

  • Q: Pellagra treatment

A: Nicotinamide 300 mg/day divided (NO FLUSH)

  • Q: Nicotinic acid vs nicotinamide

A: Nicotinic acid: flush (PGD2) + lipid lowering; Nicotinamide: NO flush, NO lipid effect

  • Q: INH prophylaxis

A: Pyridoxine 10–25 mg/day from start prevents B6 + B3 deficiency

  • Q: EGRAC

A: Erythrocyte glutathione reductase activity coefficient; >1.2–1.4 = riboflavin deficiency

18. 18. Answer Key with Explanations

1. MCQs

  1. B — FMN is the prosthetic group for Complex I (NADH dehydrogenase); FAD for Complex II.
  2. B — Superficial corneal vascularisation is pathognomonic for riboflavin deficiency.
  3. C — 60 mg tryptophan yields 1 mg niacin (requires B2, B6, Fe).
  4. B — Casal's necklace = photosensitive hyperpigmented collar rash in pellagra.
  5. B — Hartnup = SLC6A19 (B⁰AT1) neutral amino acid transporter defect; autosomal recessive.
  6. B — Carcinoid: tryptophan → 5-HTP → serotonin (via TPH) → ↑5-HIAA; tryptophan diverted from niacin pathway.
  7. B — INH hydrazine metabolite inhibits pyridoxal kinase → ↓PLP → kynureninase inhibition → ↓niacin synthesis.
  8. B — Nicotinamide (niacinamide) 300 mg/day treats pellagra without causing prostaglandin D2-mediated flush.
  9. B — Pyridoxine 10–25 mg/day with INH prevents B6 deficiency and secondary niacin deficiency.
  10. B — Nicotinic acid causes flush (PGD2 via GPER109A) and lowers lipids; nicotinamide has neither effect.

2. SBAs

  1. B — Hartnup: neutral AA malabsorption (SLC6A19) → tryptophan deficiency → pellagra + cerebellar ataxia, nystagmus; urinary neutral AA ↑.
  2. B — Carcinoid: serotonin-secreting NET diverts tryptophan from niacin pathway → secondary pellagra.
  3. B — INH prophylaxis: pyridoxine 10–25 mg/day prevents B6 deficiency (neuropathy) and secondary B3 deficiency (pellagra).
  4. B — Cheilosis, angular stomatitis, magenta glossitis, corneal vascularisation = classic riboflavin (B2) deficiency (C-4 mnemonic).
  5. B — Aspirin 325 mg 30 min before nicotinic acid inhibits PGD2 synthesis → reduces flush; laropiprant withdrawn.

19. 19. Summary

Riboflavin (B2) & Niacin (B3) are Must Know 🔴 topics for FCPS/MRCP.

Key takeaway: Riboflavin → FMN (Complex I) + FAD (Complex II, MTHFR, GSH reductase); deficiency = cheilosis, angular stomatitis, magenta glossitis, corneal vascularisation, normocytic anaemia. Niacin → NAD/NADP; tryptophan 60:1 (needs B2, B6, Fe); pellagra = 3 D's (Dermatitis Casal's necklace, Diarrhoea, Dementia, Death). Secondary: Hartnup (SLC6A19 neutral AA → tryptophan malabsorption + cerebellar ataxia), carcinoid (tryptophan → serotonin), INH (B6 depletion → kynureninase inhibition). Treatment: Nicotinamide 300 mg/day (no flush); INH prophylaxis: pyridoxine 10–25 mg/day.

Exam focus: Riboflavin C-4 signs, pellagra 3 D's + Casal's necklace, Hartnup + cerebellar signs, carcinoid tryptophan diversion, INH dual B6/B3 deficiency, nicotinamide vs nicotinic acid.

Clinical relevance: Alcohol malnutrition = multiple B-vitamin deficiencies; TB treatment monitoring; carcinoid syndrome workup; genetic transporter disorders.

Template version: 1.0 | Davidson 24e Ch 22 aligned | FCPS/MRCP oriented

topics.title: Thiamine B1 Beriberi Wernicke Korsakoff
topics.html:
Related: [[Nutritional Factors in Disease MOC]], [[Davidson Chapter 22 - Nutritional Factors in Disease Hierarchy]], [[../00_Index/Medicine MOC|Medicine MOC]]

[!important]
Thiamine (B1) = cofactor for pyruvate dehydrogenase, α-ketoglutarate dehydrogenase, transketolase; deficiency = beriberi (wet/dry) + Wernicke-Korsakoff; high-risk: alcoholism, malnutrition, hyperemesis, TPN, bariatric.

1. 1. Learning Objectives

  • [ ] Describe thiamine structure, absorption (active transport + passive), phosphorylation to TPP (active cofactor)
  • [ ] List TPP-dependent enzymes: pyruvate dehydrogenase, α-ketoglutarate dehydrogenase, transketolase (PPP), branched-chain ketoacid dehydrogenase
  • [ ] Explain energy metabolism defect: ↓ATP, ↑lactate, ↑pyruvate, impaired glucose oxidation
  • [ ] Differentiate wet beriberi (high-output cardiac failure) vs dry beriberi (peripheral neuropathy)
  • [ ] Recognise Wernicke's triad: ophthalmoplegia, ataxia, confusion (+ Korsakoff: anterograde amnesia, confabulation)
  • [ ] State treatment: IV thiamine 500 mg TDS ×3–5 days (Wernicke) / 100–200 mg/day (beriberi); give BEFORE glucose
  • [ ] Identify high-risk: alcohol, hyperemesis gravidarum, TPN without vitamins, bariatric, dialysis, HIV, cancer

2. 2. Definitions / Key Concepts

TermDefinition
Thiamine (B1)Water-soluble vitamin; pyrimidine + thiazole ring; absorbed jejunum (active transport low dose, passive high dose)
TPP (Thiamine Pyrophosphate)Active cofactor (thiamine diphosphate); formed by thiamine pyrophosphokinase (TPK1) in cytosol; Mg²⁺ required
TransketolasePPP enzyme; requires TPP; activity in RBCs = functional thiamine status (Etk activity coefficient >1.25 = deficiency)
Pyruvate Dehydrogenase (PDH)Pyruvate → acetyl-CoA; TPP + lipoamide + CoA + FAD + NAD⁺; ↑pyruvate → lactate if deficient
α-Ketoglutarate DehydrogenaseKrebs cycle; α-KG → succinyl-CoA; TPP-dependent; impaired → ↑α-KG, ↓ATP
Branched-Chain Ketoacid DehydrogenaseBCAA catabolism; TPP-dependent; deficiency → maple syrup urine disease
Wet BeriberiHigh-output cardiac failure: tachycardia, oedema, cardiomegaly, warm peripheries, ↑CO, ↓SVR
Dry BeriberiSymmetrical sensorimotor polyneuropathy: glove-stocking loss, areflexia, foot drop, wasting
Shoshin BeriberiAcute fulminant cardiovascular beriberi: hypotension, metabolic acidosis, oliguria, high mortality
Wernicke's EncephalopathyAcute: ophthalmoplegia (nystagmus, lateral gaze palsy), gait ataxia, confusion; MRI: mamillary bodies, periaqueductal
Korsakoff's SyndromeChronic: anterograde amnesia, confabulation, retrograde amnesia; mamillary body atrophy; often irreversible
Wernicke-Korsakoff SyndromeContinuum: acute Wernicke → chronic Korsakoff if untreated; treat Wernicke urgently to prevent progression
High-Output Failure↑CO, ↓SVR, warm shock; thiamine deficiency → arteriolar vasodilation (lactate/pyruvate ratio)
Thiamine Responsive DisordersThiamine transporter 2 (SLC19A2) mutation → thiamine-responsive megaloblastic anaemia (TRMA)

3. 3. Core Content

1. Section 1: Metabolism & Biochemistry

Absorption: Duodenum/jejunum; low concentrations — active transport (SLC19A2, SLC19A3, RFC); high concentrations — passive diffusion; inhibited by alcohol, tea polyphenols, raw fish (thiaminase).

Phosphorylation: Thiamine + ATP → TPP (thiamine pyrophosphokinase, TPK1); Mg²⁺ cofactor; occurs in liver, brain, kidney, heart.

Excretion: Renal (free thiamine, metabolites); saturable reabsorption; half-life 1–2 weeks; body stores ~30 mg (depleted in 2–3 weeks zero intake).

TPP-Dependent Enzymes:

  1. Pyruvate dehydrogenase (PDH): Pyruvate + NAD⁺ + CoA → acetyl-CoA + NADH + CO₂; links glycolysis to Krebs
  2. α-Ketoglutarate dehydrogenase (α-KGDH): α-KG + NAD⁺ + CoA → succinyl-CoA + NADH + CO₂; Krebs cycle
  3. Transketolase (TKT): Pentose phosphate pathway; xylulose-5-P + ribose-5-P ↔ glyceraldehyde-3-P + sedoheptulose-7-P; NADPH + ribose for nucleotides
  4. Branched-chain ketoacid dehydrogenase (BCKDH): BCAA (leucine, isoleucine, valine) catabolism

2. Section 2: Beriberi — Clinical Features

TypeFeaturesPathophysiology
Wet (Cardiac)Tachycardia, high-output failure, peripheral oedema (pitting), cardiomegaly, warm peripheries, wide pulse pressure, ↑JVP, gallop rhythm, pleural effusion↓ATP in myocardium; arteriolar vasodilation (↑lactate/pyruvate) → ↓SVR → high CO; sodium retention
Dry (Neurological)Symmetrical distal sensorimotor polyneuropathy: glove-stocking sensory loss, burning feet, areflexia, foot drop, wrist drop, wastingAxonal degeneration (↓ATP for axonal transport); posterior column + spinocerebellar involvement
Shoshin (Acute Fulminant)Rapid cardiovascular collapse: hypotension, tachycardia, metabolic acidosis (lactic), oliguria, altered mental status, high mortalitySevere myocardial dysfunction + lactic acidosis; requires urgent IV thiamine + supportive

Infantile beriberi: 2–4 months (breastfed by thiamine-deficient mother); aphonia, vomiting, tachycardia, convulsions, heart failure; acute, fatal if untreated.

3. Section 3: Wernicke-Korsakoff Syndrome

Wernicke's Triad (classic, only 10–30% have all three):

  1. Ophthalmoplegia: Nystagmus (horizontal, gaze-evoked), lateral gaze palsy (abducens), ptosis, conjugate gaze palsy
  2. Gait ataxia: Broad-based, unsteady, truncal; due to cerebellar vermis + vestibular nuclei involvement
  3. Confusion: Global confusion, apathy, inattention, disorientation

MRI findings: Symmetrical T2/FLAIR hyperintensity in mamillary bodies, periaqueductal grey, medial thalami, floor of 4th ventricle; mammillary body atrophy (chronic).

Korsakoff's Syndrome: Anterograde amnesia (cannot form new memories), confabulation (fabricated memories to fill gaps), retrograde amnesia; relative preservation of other cognitive domains; mammillary body atrophy.

Treatment (Wernicke):

  • IV thiamine 500 mg TDS ×3–5 days (UK: Pabrinex 2 pairs TDS = 250 mg thiamine/dose; some guidelines 200 mg TDS)
  • Give BEFORE any glucose (glucose metabolism consumes residual thiamine → precipitates Wernicke)
  • Then oral 100 mg/day maintenance
  • Mg²⁺ repletion (cofactor for TPK1)

4. Section 4: High-Risk Groups & Causes

GroupMechanism
Alcohol use disorder↓Intake, ↓absorption, ↓phosphorylation (liver disease), ↑excretion, ↑demand
Hyperemesis gravidarumProlonged vomiting, nil by mouth, high glucose IV without vitamins
TPN without vitaminsNo thiamine in standard bags; must add multivitamin
Bariatric surgery (RYGB)Bypassed duodenum (absorption site), vomiting, restricted intake
DialysisLoss in dialysate, dietary restriction, comorbid illness
HIV / Cancer / Critical illnessHypermetabolic, ↓intake, malabsorption
Diuretic use (furosemide)↑Renal thiamine excretion
Raw fish / fermented teaThiaminase (raw fish), anti-thiamine factors (tea polyphenols)

5. Section 5: Diagnosis

Functional test: Erythrocyte transketolase activity (Etk); Etk activity coefficient = Etk + TPP / Etk baseline; >1.25 = deficiency.

Direct levels: Whole blood thiamine (ref 70–180 nmol/L) or plasma (ref 7–30 nmol/L); whole blood more reliable.

Supportive: ↑Blood lactate, ↑pyruvate, ↑lactate/pyruvate ratio (>25), metabolic acidosis; ↓branched-chain amino acids.

MRI: Mamillary bodies, periaqueductal, medial thalami (Wernicke).

6. Section 6: Treatment Protocols

ConditionThiamine DoseRouteDuration
Wernicke's Encephalopathy500 mg TDS (or 200–250 mg TDS)IV3–5 days, then oral 100 mg/day
Wet Beriberi100–200 mg/dayIV/IMUntil improvement, then oral
Dry Beriberi50–100 mg/dayOral/IVWeeks–months (neuropathy slow)
Prophylaxis (High-risk)100 mg/dayIV/PODuring risk period
Infantile Beriberi25–50 mgIV/IMUrgent; mother also treated

Critical: ALWAYS give thiamine BEFORE glucose in at-risk patients (alcohol, malnutrition, hyperemesis). Glucose → ↑pyruvate → ↑TPP demand → precipitates Wernicke.

4. 4. Clinical Correlation

ScenarioActionNotes
45M, alcohol detox, confusion, nystagmus, ataxiaIV thiamine 500 mg TDS ×3–5d BEFORE glucose; Mg²⁺; PabrinexWernicke's; do NOT wait for MRI; treat empirically
30F, hyperemesis 4w, IV dextrose started, now confusedSTOP glucose; IV thiamine 500 mg STAT; continue TDS; check Mg²⁺Glucose precipitated Wernicke; urgent
60M, RYGB 6m ago, vomiting, numb feet, areflexiaThiamine 100 mg IV daily ×5d then oral; check B12, folate, Cu, FeBariatric = multivitamin deficiency screen
8m infant, breastfed, lethargic, tachycardia, oedemaThiamine 25 mg IV STAT; mother also 100 mg/day; echoInfantile beriberi; maternal deficiency
55M on furosemide 160 mg/day, tachycardia, oedema, ↑lactateThiamine 100 mg IV daily; check Mg²⁺, K⁺; consider wet beriberiFurosemide ↑thiamine excretion
70M, TPN 2w, no multivitamin, encephalopathyAdd MVI to TPN; IV thiamine 100 mg/day ×5dTPN without vitamins = beriberi risk

5. 5. High-Yield FCPS/MRCP Points

[!important]
- Must know: TPP-dependent enzymes (PDH, α-KGDH, transketolase, BCKDH); wet vs dry beriberi; Wernicke triad (ophthalmoplegia, ataxia, confusion); Korsakoff (amnesia, confabulation); thiamine BEFORE glucose; IV 500 mg TDS for Wernicke; high-risk: alcohol, hyperemesis, TPN, bariatric, dialysis; MRI: mamillary bodies
- Common viva: Why thiamine before glucose? Wet vs dry beriberi pathophysiology; Wernicke vs Korsakoff; Etk activity coefficient; Shoshin beriberi; alcohol mechanisms; infantile beriberi
- Exam trap: Giving glucose before thiamine in alcoholic; missing Wernicke (only 1/3 have full triad); treating Korsakoff as reversible; using oral thiamine for acute Wernicke; confusing beriberi with Guillain-Barré (reflexes preserved early in GBS)

6. 6. Common Confusions / Exam Traps

TrapCorrection
Glucose first, then thiamineThiamine MUST precede glucose — glucose consumes residual TPP, precipitates Wernicke
Oral thiamine for WernickeIV required (absorption impaired, urgent); 500 mg TDS ×3–5d
Korsakoff reversibleLargely irreversible; treat Wernicke URGENTLY to prevent progression
All 3 features needed for WernickeOnly 10–30% have full triad; treat on clinical suspicion (2/3 features)
Wet beriberi = low-output failureHIGH-output failure (↑CO, ↓SVR, warm peripheries); Shoshin = acute fulminant
Thiamine deficiency = only alcoholAlso: hyperemesis, TPN, bariatric, dialysis, furosemide, HIV, cancer
Beriberi neuropathy = Guillain-BarréBeriberi: symmetrical, sensory>motor, gradual; GBS: ascending, areflexia, albuminocytological dissociation
TPP = thiamine monophosphateTPP = thiamine diphosphate (pyrophosphate); TMP = monophosphate (inactive)
Etk coefficient <1.25 = normal>1.25 = deficiency (apoprotein saturation <80%)
MRI normal excludes WernickeEarly MRI may be normal; treat clinically; MRI sensitivity ~50% acute

7. 7. Mnemonics

  • TPP enzymes: PAT B = Pyruvate dehydrogenase, Alpha-KGDH, Transketolase, BCKDH
  • Beriberi: WET = cardiac (high-output, oedema, warm); DRY = neuropathy (glove-stocking, foot drop)
  • Wernicke triad: OAC = Ophthalmoplegia, Ataxia, Confusion (classic, but only 1/3 have all)
  • Thiamine before glucose: THIAMINE FIRST — "Don't sweeten the starving brain"
  • High-risk: HALT = Hyperemesis, Alcohol, L (TPN/bypass), T (diuretics/dialysis)
  • Wernicke MRI: MAM = Mamillary bodies, Aqueduct (periaqueductal), Medial thalami
  • Korsakoff: KORS = Korsakoff, Operative (amnesia), Retrograde, S (confabulation)
  • Infantile beriberi: INFANT = Infant, Nursing, Fulminant, Aphonia, Neonatal, Thiamine
  • Treatment dose: 500 TDS (Wernicke), 100 DAILY (beriberi/prophylaxis)

8. 8. Mind Map

mindmap
  root((Thiamine B1))
    Metabolism
      Absorption: SLC19A2/3 (jejunum)
      Phosphorylation: TPK1 + Mg²⁺ → TPP
      Stores: ~30mg (2-3 weeks zero intake)
      Excretion: Renal, saturable
    TPP-Dependent Enzymes
      Pyruvate Dehydrogenase
      Alpha-Ketoglutarate Dehydrogenase

      BCKDH (BCAA)
    Beriberi

        Tachycardia, oedema, warm peripheries
        ↓SVR, ↑CO
      Dry (Neurological)

        Glove-stocking, foot drop
      Shoshin (Acute Fulminant)

        High mortality
      Infantile
        Breastfed by deficient mother
        Aphonia, heart failure
    Wernicke-Korsakoff

        Ophthalmoplegia (nystagmus)
        Gait ataxia
        Confusion
        MRI: Mamillary bodies, periaqueductal
      Korsakoff (Chronic)

        Confabulation
        Mammillary body atrophy
        Largely irreversible
    High-Risk Groups
      Alcohol
      Hyperemesis gravidarum
      TPN (no MVI)
      Bariatric (RYGB)
      Dialysis
      Furosemide
      HIV/Cancer

      Etk activity coefficient >1.25
      Whole blood thiamine <70 nmol/L
      ↑Lactate/pyruvate ratio
